# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q: With neat diagrams, explain the construction and principle of operation of an n-channel JFET. Mention the role of the pinch-off voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

An n-channel JFET is a three-terminal unipolar device consisting of an n-type conducting channel diffused into a p-type substrate, with two heavily doped p⁺ regions forming the gate. The three terminals — Source (S), Drain (D), and Gate (G) — are ohmic contacts to the channel ends and the gate regions respectively.

**Principle of Operation:**
When $V_{DS} > 0$ is applied with $V_{GS} = 0$, electrons flow from S to D, producing the maximum drain current $I_{DSS}$. The gate-channel p-n junctions are at zero bias, with narrow depletion regions.

When a reverse bias $V_{GS} < 0$ is applied, the depletion regions grow inward from both sides, **constricting the channel cross-section**. This reduces the drain current $I_D$ because the effective conducting area is smaller. The relationship is governed by Shockley's equation:
$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

**Role of Pinch-off Voltage $V_P$:**
At a particular negative gate voltage $V_{GS} = V_P$ (typically −2 V to −10 V), the two depletion regions meet at the center of the channel, completely cutting off the conducting path. The drain current drops to **zero**. This critical voltage is called the **pinch-off voltage** $V_P$ (also denoted $V_{GS(\text{off})}$). It defines the cutoff boundary of the device and is a key parameter in determining the Q-point and small-signal $g_m$.

**(Word count: ~200; diagrams: JFET cross-section + symbol; 3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q: Compare the constructional features and $V_{GS} = 0$ behavior of E-MOSFET and D-MOSFET.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | E-MOSFET (Enhancement) | D-MOSFET (Depletion) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical channel at fabrication | **No** — must be induced by gate voltage | **Yes** — channel is physically present |
| Conduction at $V_{GS} = 0$ | **OFF** ($I_D = 0$); needs $V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$ | **ON** ($I_D = I_{DSS}$); conducts with zero gate bias |
| Gate oxide thickness | Standard thin SiO₂ | Thin SiO₂, but channel is below the oxide |
| Operating mode | Enhancement only (for n-channel) | Both depletion ($V_{GS} < 0$) and enhancement ($V_{GS} > 0$) |
| Transfer equation (sat) | $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$ | $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$ |
| Typical use | Digital CMOS logic, switching | RF amplifiers, constant-current sources |

**Constructional difference:** The D-MOSFET is fabricated by diffusing a thin n-channel between source and drain **before** growing the gate oxide; the E-MOSFET omits this step, leaving the source-drain region as p-substrate with two n⁺ wells. Both use the SiO₂ layer to insulate the gate.

**(Word count: ~180; table: 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Option A — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the structure and physical operation of an n-channel enhancement-type MOSFET. Discuss the formation of the inversion layer. (7 marks)
>
> **Q (b)** For an n-channel E-MOSFET with $k = 0.4 \text{ mA/V}^2$ and $V_{GS(th)} = 2 \text{ V}$, biased at $V_{GS} = 5 \text{ V}$, find: (i) drain current in saturation, (ii) transconductance $g_m$, (iii) the overdrive voltage, and (iv) the device's output resistance if $\lambda = 0.02 \text{ V}^{-1}$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (a):**

The n-channel E-MOSFET is fabricated on a **p-type silicon substrate**. Two heavily-doped n⁺ regions — the **source** and **drain** — are diffused into the substrate, separated by a small distance (typically 1–10 µm). A thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** of about 0.1 µm thickness is thermally grown over the region between S and D. A **metal (or polysilicon) gate electrode** is deposited on top of the oxide. Ohmic contacts are made to source, drain, gate, and (often) the substrate (body).

**Physical Operation — Formation of the Inversion Layer:**

When $V_{GS} = 0$, the region between S and D is simply p-type substrate. Source and drain are n⁺ regions forming two back-to-back p-n junctions. With $V_{DS} > 0$ applied, one junction is forward-biased and the other is reverse-biased; the resulting current is essentially zero (only leakage). The device is **OFF**.

As $V_{GS}$ is increased positively:
1. **Holes are repelled** from the region immediately under the oxide (the Si-SiO₂ interface). A depletion region of negatively charged acceptor ions forms.
2. As $V_{GS}$ increases further, **minority electrons** (from the p-substrate) are attracted toward the oxide interface. At $V_{GS} = V_{GS(th)}$ (the **threshold voltage**), the electron concentration at the surface **exceeds** the hole concentration — this is called **inversion**, and a thin conducting layer of electrons forms under the oxide.
3. This thin electron layer is the **inversion layer** — it forms a continuous n-type channel connecting the source and drain n⁺ regions.
4. For $V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$, the channel becomes more conducting, and drain current $I_D$ flows.

**Modes of operation:**
- $V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$: **Cutoff** — $I_D = 0$
- $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$: **Triode** — $I_D = k[2(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2]$
- $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$: **Saturation** — $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$

The saturation condition occurs when the channel is "pinched off" at the drain end — analogous to the JFET pinch-off, but here the channel is **induced** rather than physical. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for operation stages, 2 for regions)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (b):**

**Given:** $k = 0.4 \text{ mA/V}^2$, $V_{GS(th)} = 2 \text{ V}$, $V_{GS} = 5 \text{ V}$, $\lambda = 0.02 \text{ V}^{-1}$.

**(i) Drain current in saturation:**
$$I_D = k (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 = 0.4 \times (5 - 2)^2 = 0.4 \times 9 = 3.6 \text{ mA}$$

**(ii) Transconductance $g_m$:**
$$g_m = 2k(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}) = 2 \times 0.4 \times 3 = 2.4 \text{ mA/V}$$

Alternative: $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = 2 \times 3.6 / 3 = 2.4 \text{ mA/V}$. ✓

**(iii) Overdrive voltage:**
$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Output resistance:**
$$r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{1}{0.02 \times 3.6 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{1}{0.072 \text{ mA/V}} = 13.89 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 1 for (iii), 2 for (iv))**

---

### Question B-B (Option B — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q (a)** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of a MOSFET in saturation. Using this, derive the expression for the voltage gain of a common-source amplifier with a bypassed source resistor. (7 marks)
>
> **Q (b)** A CS MOSFET amplifier has $g_m = 2 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 500 \text{ k}\Omega$. The MOSFET has $r_o = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the voltage gain $A_v$, (ii) input impedance $Z_{in}$, (iii) output impedance $Z_{out}$, and (iv) the overall voltage gain if a 100 kΩ load is connected at the output. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (a):**

**Small-Signal Model of the MOSFET in Saturation:**

For low-frequency analysis, the MOSFET is replaced by a **hybrid-π equivalent circuit**:
- The gate-source port is modeled as an **open circuit** (input resistance $\to \infty$).
- A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ is connected between drain and source, pointing from D to S (positive at the drain when $v_{gs} > 0$).
- An output resistance $r_o$ is connected between D and S in parallel with the controlled source (to model channel-length modulation).

**CS Amplifier with Bypassed $R_S$:**

The CS amplifier (with $C_S$ in place) presents a small-signal source resistance of zero from the source terminal to ground (since $C_S$ shorts $R_S$ at signal frequencies). Therefore $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ directly.

The small-signal drain current is:
$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} = g_m v_{in}$$

The output voltage at the drain (with the small-signal source $v_{dd} = 0$ for AC analysis) is:
$$v_{out} = - i_d \cdot (R_D \parallel r_o) = - g_m v_{in} (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

(The negative sign arises because an increase in $i_d$ causes a larger drop across $R_D$, leaving less voltage at the drain relative to ground.)

The voltage gain is:
$$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

If $r_o \gg R_D$ (often the case in discrete designs):
$$A_v \approx -g_m R_D$$

This is the **standard CS amplifier gain formula** with 180° phase inversion. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for derivation steps, 3 for final result and interpretation)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (b):**

**Given:** $g_m = 2 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 500 \text{ k}\Omega$, $r_o = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**(i) Voltage gain $A_v$:**
$$R_D \parallel r_o = \dfrac{5 \times 50}{5 + 50} = \dfrac{250}{55} = 4.545 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -2 \text{ mA/V} \times 4.545 \text{ k}\Omega = -9.09 \text{ V/V}$$

**(ii) Input impedance $Z_{in}$:**
$$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \dfrac{1000 \times 500}{1500} = 333.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**(iii) Output impedance $Z_{out}$:**
$$Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 4.545 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**(iv) Overall voltage gain with 100 kΩ load $R_L$ connected at output:**

The effective load becomes:
$$R_{L,\text{eff}} = R_D \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = 4.545 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$= \dfrac{4.545 \times 100}{104.545} = 4.347 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_{v,\text{loaded}} = -g_m R_{L,\text{eff}} = -2 \text{ mA/V} \times 4.347 \text{ k}\Omega = -8.69 \text{ V/V}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for (i), 1 for (ii), 1 for (iii), 3 for (iv))**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention for $V_P$:** Forgetting that $V_P$ is **negative** for n-channel JFETs. Substituting $V_P = +4$ instead of $V_P = -4$ yields a wrong $I_D$. **(–1 to –2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Square-law equation for E-MOSFET:** Many students write $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 / 2$ (with the factor of 2) instead of the standard form $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$ where $k$ already includes the ½. **Always check how $k$ is defined in your paper.** **(–1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Saturation condition verification:** Forgetting to verify that $V_{DSQ} > V_{GSQ} - V_{GS(th)}$ after computing the Q-point. If violated, the device is in triode, and the bias design is wrong. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 4. **CS amplifier gain sign:** Omitting the **negative sign** in $A_v = -g_m R_D$. The phase inversion is a critical feature of CS amplifiers and is often a 1-mark question by itself. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Voltage divider analysis with $R_S$ non-zero:** Students often forget to compute $V_G = V_{GS} + I_D R_S$ (not $V_{GS}$ alone) when $R_S$ is present. The gate voltage is **above** $V_{GS}$ by the source-bias drop. **(–1 to –2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Pinch-off vs. saturation terminology confusion:** "Pinch-off" describes the JFET channel condition (physical depletion), whereas "saturation" is the operating region. In MOSFETs, "pinch-off" is sometimes used loosely to mean the onset of saturation — be precise. **(–1 mark for examiners who are strict on terminology)**
>
> 7. **E-MOSFET $I_D = 0$ at $V_{GS} = 0$:** Some students assume $I_{DSS}$ exists for E-MOSFETs like JFETs. It does not — the E-MOSFET is OFF at $V_{GS} = 0$. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **JFET** = Junction FET, gate is a reverse-biased p-n junction; normally ON at $V_{GS} = 0$.
>
> ✅ **MOSFET** = Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor FET, gate is insulated by SiO₂; D-MOSFET normally ON, E-MOSFET normally OFF.
>
> ✅ **Shockley's equation** (JFET and D-MOSFET, in saturation): $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$.
>
> ✅ **MOSFET square law** (E-MOSFET, in saturation): $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$, where $k = \mu_n C_{ox} W/(2L)$.
>
> ✅ **Three operating regions** for MOSFET: Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$), Triode ($V_{DS} < V_{OV}$), Saturation ($V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$).
>
> ✅ **Transconductance $g_m$** is the **slope** of the transfer curve; the most important small-signal parameter.
>
> ✅ **JFET $g_m$:** $g_m = g_{m0}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P) = (2/\vert V_P \vert)\sqrt{I_D I_{DSS}}$.
>
> ✅ **MOSFET $g_m$:** $g_m = 2k V_{OV} = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = \sqrt{2k I_D}$.
>
> ✅ **Voltage-divider bias** is the most stable MOSFET bias configuration, independent of $I_{DSS}$ and $V_P$ variations.
>
> ✅ **Self-bias** is used for JFETs: $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$, solved with Shockley's equation.
>
> ✅ **CS amplifier gain** (bypassed $R_S$): $A_v = -g_m(R_D \parallel r_o) \approx -g_m R_D$.
>
> ✅ **CS amplifier gain** (unbypassed $R_S$): $A_v = -g_m R_D / (1 + g_m R_S)$.
>
> ✅ **Phase inversion:** CS amplifier produces 180° phase shift between input and output.
>
> ✅ **Input impedance of CS amp** (with voltage-divider bias): $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2$.
>
> ✅ **Output impedance of CS amp**: $Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o \approx R_D$.
>
> ✅ **Saturation verification** (always check after Q-point calculation): $V_{DSQ} > V_{OV} = V_{GSQ} - V_{GS(th)}$.
>
> ✅ **Output resistance** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_{DQ})$ — accounts for channel-length modulation.
>
> ✅ **Real-world numbers:** E-MOSFET $V_{GS(th)} \approx 0.5$–$2$ V; $k \approx 0.1$–$1$ mA/V²; $\lambda \approx 0.01$–$0.05$ V⁻¹.
>
> ✅ **Master formula** linking all small-signal parameters: $A_v \cdot \text{BW} = g_m / (2\pi C_{tot})$ — the **gain-bandwidth product** (GBW) of the amplifier.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifiers: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Amplifiers: Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal electronic amplifier designed to deliver appreciable power to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, or motor) with acceptable efficiency and minimal distortion. Unlike small-signal (voltage) amplifiers, power amplifiers are characterized by their ability to handle high currents and large voltage swings simultaneously, and they are conventionally **classified based on the conduction angle (θ) of the output transistor(s)** over one complete cycle of the input signal (360°).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Classification of an amplifier is determined by the **portion of the input cycle (conduction angle)** during which the output transistor conducts current. This directly controls the quiescent bias point (Q-point) location on the load line.

### 1.2 The Five Major Classes of Power Amplifiers

| Class | Conduction Angle (θ) | Q-Point Location | Typical Use-Case |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Class A** | 360° | Center of load line | High-fidelity audio pre-driver stages |
| **Class B** | 180° | Cut-off region | Audio push-pull output stages |
| **Class AB** | 180° < θ < 360° | Slightly above cut-off | Hi-Fi audio output (most common) |
| **Class C** | θ < 180° | Deep below cut-off | RF tuned amplifiers, IF stages |
| **Class D** | Switching (PWM) | Saturation / Cut-off | Subwoofers, SMPS, motor drivers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Class C amplifiers are intentionally operated in the KTU Module-3 syllabus context as RF/Tuned amplifiers**, not audio. They MUST be followed by a tuned (LC) tank circuit to reconstruct the sinusoid.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a garden water tap controlling flow to a turbine:

* **Class A** = The tap is **half-open, always flowing**, even when no work is needed. Wastes water (power) but gives smoothest, most linear control. *(High fidelity, low efficiency)*
* **Class B** = Two taps working in **push-pull** — one handles the *push* (positive half), the other the *pull* (negative half). Each tap is shut completely when not in use. *(Good efficiency, slight crossover "hiccup")*
* **Class AB** = Both taps are **slightly pre-opened** to remove the crossover hiccup, but mostly shut when idle. *(Best compromise for audio)*
* **Class C** = The tap opens only for **a short burst** in each cycle, relying on a flywheel (LC tank) to keep the turbine spinning smoothly. *(Very efficient but distorted — needs filtering)*
* **Class D** = The tap is either **fully ON or fully OFF** at high speed (PWM), with an LC filter converting the pulses back to a smooth flow. *(Theoretically lossless switching)*

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in This Module

* **Conduction Angle (θ)** — measured in **degrees** or **radians**.
* **Conversion Efficiency (η)** = (AC power delivered to load) / (DC power drawn from supply) — expressed in **%**.
* **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency (η_max)** — the upper bound assuming ideal transistor and ideal biasing.
* **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — measured in **%**.

> [!TIP]
> A useful rule-of-thumb to memorize for KTU viva: *Class A → 25% (series-fed) / 50% (transformer-coupled); Class B → 78.5% ($\pi/4$); Class C → 100% (theoretical limit, with tuned load).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Classification — The Q-Point Philosophy

The location of the Q-point on the DC load line is the **fundamental physical reason** for the existence of amplifier classes:

* **Low Q-point near cut-off** → Transistor conducts for less than half cycle → **Less DC power wasted** → **Higher efficiency**.
* **High Q-point near saturation** → Transistor conducts fully → **More DC power consumed** → **Lower efficiency** but **linear amplification**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Axiom:** You cannot simultaneously achieve (a) 100% efficiency, (b) zero distortion, and (c) linearity. The amplifier class is your designer's *dial* balancing these three competing demands.

### 2.2 Class A — Single-Transistor, Full-Cycle Conduction

* **Bias arrangement:** Q-point is set at the **center of the DC load line** using a resistive divider (or transformer-coupled).
* **Conduction:** Output transistor conducts for the **entire 360°** of the input cycle.
* **Distortion:** Lowest of all classes — essentially the most linear.
* **Efficiency ceiling:** **25%** for series-fed (resistive load) and **50%** for ideal transformer-coupled.
* **Drawback:** Even with no input signal, the transistor drains continuous DC current → enormous heat dissipation.

### 2.3 Class B — Push-Pull, Half-Cycle Conduction

* **Bias arrangement:** Q-point is at **cut-off** (V_BE = 0). Two complementary transistors (NPN + PNP, or matched pair) form a push-pull pair.
* **Conduction:** NPN handles the **positive half cycle**, PNP handles the **negative half cycle**. Each conducts for exactly **180°**.
* **Distortion:** Suffers from **crossover distortion** near 0 V because real BJTs require ~0.6–0.7 V to turn ON.
* **Efficiency ceiling:** **78.5%** ($\pi/4$).

### 2.4 Class AB — The Crossover Killer

* **Bias arrangement:** Q-point is **slightly above cut-off** (V_BE ≈ 0.5–0.7 V for BJTs) so that both transistors are *just barely* conducting at idle.
* **Conduction angle:** Between **180° and 360°**.
* **Distortion:** Crossover distortion is virtually eliminated.
* **Efficiency:** Practically between 50% and 78.5%.
* **Use:** Dominant choice for **Hi-Fi audio amplifiers** and op-amp output stages.

### 2.5 Class C — Highly Efficient, Highly Distorted

* **Bias arrangement:** Q-point is **well below cut-off** (reverse bias on B-E junction).
* **Conduction angle:** **Less than 180°** (typically 90°–150°).
* **Output stage:** MUST use a **parallel resonant LC tank** to reconstruct the sine wave (acts as a flywheel).
* **Efficiency ceiling:** Approaches **100%** as θ → 0.
* **Application:** RF power amplifiers in transmitters, IF amplifiers in superheterodyne receivers.

### 2.6 Class D — Switching (PWM) Amplifier

* **Operation:** Transistor operates as a **switch** (either fully ON in saturation or fully OFF in cut-off). The audio input is converted to a Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM) waveform.
* **Output stage:** Low-pass LC filter reconstructs the analog audio.
* **Efficiency:** Theoretically up to **100%** (since the switch dissipates P = V·I = 0 in both states).
* **Use:** Subwoofers, battery-powered amplifiers, modern SMPS audio.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Class A (Series-fed) | Class A (Transformer) | Class B (Push-Pull) | Class C (Tuned) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Conduction Angle θ | **360°** | **360°** | **180°** | **< 180°** | 
| DC Input Power $P_{DC}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $\dfrac{2 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot V_p}{\pi \cdot R_L}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$ |
| AC Output Power $P_{ac}$ | $\dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$ |
| Conversion Efficiency $\eta$ | $\dfrac{V_p}{2 V_{CC}}$ | $\dfrac{V_p}{V_{CC}}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot V_p}{2 V_{CC}}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot V_p}{2 V_{CC}} \cdot \dfrac{\theta - \sin\theta}{\theta \cdot (1 - \cos(\theta/2))}$ |
| $\eta_{max}$ (theoretical) | **25 %** | **50 %** | **78.5 %** ($\pi/4$) | **≈ 100 %** |
| Crossover Distortion | None | None | **YES** (eliminated by Class AB) | N/A (tuned load) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the Class C formula above, the term $\dfrac{\theta - \sin\theta}{2 \sin(\theta/2) - \theta \cos(\theta/2)}$ describes the relationship between conduction angle and efficiency. For KTU exam, only the limiting cases (θ = 360° → Class A, θ = 180° → Class B, θ → 0 → 100%) are usually tested.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Audio Hi-Fi amplifiers** (home theater, PA systems) → **Class AB push-pull** with thermal feedback.
* **Mobile phone speakers** → **Class D** with > 90% efficiency to save battery.
* **RF transmitter final stage** (cell tower, radio broadcast) → **Class C** with high-Q cavity resonator.
* **Headphone amplifiers** → **Class A** (low-power, ultra-low distortion).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Efficiency of Class A (Series-Fed)

**Given:** A series-fed Class A amplifier with supply $V_{CC}$, collector resistor acting as load, and Q-point set at the midpoint of the load line for maximum symmetrical swing.

**Step 1 — Establish the Q-point and maximum signal amplitude.**

The quiescent collector current and voltage are:

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{CEQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

For maximum unclipped swing, the peak output voltage $V_p$ equals the quiescent value:

$$V_p = V_{CEQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the DC power drawn from the supply.**

The DC supply delivers a constant current equal to the quiescent current $I_{CQ}$:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the AC (signal) power delivered to the load.**

For a sinusoidal output $v_o(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$, the average power across $R_L$ is:

$$P_{ac} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(V_{CC}/2)^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 / (8 R_L)}{V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_L)} = \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 25\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means **75% of the DC power is wasted as heat** even in the best possible Class A design. This is why Class A amplifiers require large heat sinks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Efficiency of Class B (Push-Pull)

**Given:** A complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier with two BJTs (NPN + PNP), each biased at cut-off, supply rails $\pm V_{CC}$, and load $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Write the expression for instantaneous output current.**

Each transistor conducts for half a cycle. The load current is a full sine wave:

$$i_L(t) = I_p \sin(\omega t)$$

where $I_p = V_p / R_L$ is the peak load current, and $V_p$ is the peak output voltage.

**Step 2 — Compute the average (DC) current drawn from the supply.**

A single transistor conducts for half the cycle, so its average current is:

$$I_{DC, \text{per transistor}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_p \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{I_p}{\pi}$$

Since the two transistors alternately connect the load to $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$, the **total** average current from both supplies is:

$$I_{DC, \text{total}} = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_p}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total DC power drawn from the supplies.**

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC, \text{total}} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the AC (signal) power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{ac} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_p^2 / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_p / (\pi R_L)}$$

After algebraic simplification, the $R_L$ terms and one factor of $V_p$ cancel:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot V_p}{4 V_{CC}}$$

**Step 6 — Maximize the efficiency.**

The efficiency is maximum when the output swing is maximum, i.e., $V_p = V_{CC}$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi \cdot V_{CC}}{4 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 78.5\%}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Power Dissipation in Class B

**Given:** The same Class B push-pull amplifier. Find the transistor power dissipation $P_D$ as a function of $V_p$ and locate its peak.

**Step 1 — Express total power dissipation as DC power minus AC power.**

By conservation of energy, the power dissipated as heat in the two transistors is:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the formulas from Derivation 2.**

$$P_D = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $V_p$ to find the maximum.**

$$\frac{d P_D}{d V_p} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_p}{R_L} = 0$$

Solving for $V_p$:

$$V_{p, \text{peak dissipation}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find the maximum dissipation.**

$$P_{D, \text{max}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \right)^2$$

$$P_{D, \text{max}} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{2 \pi^2 R_L} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

$$P_{D, \text{max}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx \frac{0.405 \cdot V_{CC}^2}{R_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **maximum thermal load** a Class B transistor pair must survive. It occurs when $V_p = 2 V_{CC}/\pi$ (NOT at maximum output, where $V_p = V_{CC}$). KTU problems frequently test this.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — The "At-a-Glance" Comparison

| Property | Class A | Class B | Class AB | Class C |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Q-point | Center | Cut-off | Slightly ON | Below cut-off |
| Conduction θ | 360° | 180° | 180°–360° | < 180° |
| $\eta_{max}$ (theoretical) | 25 % | 78.5 % | 50 %–78.5 % | ≈ 100 % |
| Distortion | Lowest | Crossover glitch | Very low | Highest (needs tank) |
| Heat sink | Massive | Moderate | Moderate | Smallest |
| Typical use | Pre-amps, line drivers | Output stages (with bias) | Hi-Fi audio output | RF / IF amplifiers |
| Polarization | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ | $V_{CEQ} = 0$ | $V_{CEQ} \approx 0$ | Reverse biased BE |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Crossover Distortion Magnitude (Qualitative)

In a Class B push-pull stage, the output transistor remains OFF until the input signal exceeds the cut-in voltage $V_{\gamma} \approx 0.5\text{ V}$ (silicon) or $\approx 0.1\text{ V}$ (germanium). The "dead zone" of width $2 V_{\gamma}$ appears in the transfer characteristic near zero crossing.

A **Class AB** stage pre-biases both transistors with a small forward voltage (using diodes or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier) so that the input signal begins conducting immediately at zero crossing, eliminating the dead zone.

$$\text{Dead zone width (Class B)} = 2 V_{\gamma}$$

$$\text{Dead zone width (Class AB)} \approx 0\text{ V (with proper bias)}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why does Class B exhibit crossover distortion and how is it eliminated?"* The model answer is: *Each transistor needs ~0.6 V to turn ON, creating a gap in the output near zero crossing; Class AB pre-biases the base-emitter junctions to keep both transistors on the edge of conduction.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Hierarchy Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Power Amplifier Classification] --> nodeB[Based on Conduction Angle]
    nodeB --> nodeC1[Class A]
    nodeB --> nodeC2[Class B]
    nodeB --> nodeC3[Class AB]
    nodeB --> nodeC4[Class C]
    nodeB --> nodeC5[Class D]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[360 Degree Conduction]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD2[Q Point at Center]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD3[Eta Max 25 to 50 Percent]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE1[180 Degree Conduction]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE2[Push Pull Topology]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE3[Eta Max 78.5 Percent]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE4[Has Crossover Distortion]
    nodeC3 --> nodeF1[Conduction Between 180 and 360]
    nodeC3 --> nodeF2[Q Point Slightly Above Cutoff]
    nodeC3 --> nodeF3[No Crossover Distortion]
    nodeC4 --> nodeG1[Conduction Less Than 180]
    nodeC4 --> nodeG2[Q Point Below Cutoff]
    nodeC4 --> nodeG3[Requires Tuned LC Tank]
    nodeC4 --> nodeG4[Eta Approaches 100 Percent]
    nodeC5 --> nodeH1[Switching Mode Operation]
    nodeC5 --> nodeH2[PWM Input Signal]
    nodeC5 --> nodeH3[LC Low Pass Filter Output]
    nodeC5 --> nodeH4[Used in SMPS and Subwoofers]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Class AB Push-Pull Audio Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Audio Input Signal] --> nodePre[Pre Amplifier Stage]
    nodePre --> nodeBias[Diode Bias Network]
    nodeBias --> nodeQ1[Transistor Q1 NPN]
    nodeBias --> nodeQ2[Transistor Q2 PNP]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeLoad[Speaker Load RL]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeLoad
    nodeVcc[DC Supply Plus Minus VCC] --> nodeQ1
    nodeVcc2[DC Supply Minus VCC] --> nodeQ2
    nodeLoad --> nodeOut[Amplified Audio Output]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Class C RF Tuned Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRfIn[RF Input Signal] --> nodeRfAmp[Class C Transistor]
    nodeRfAmp --> nodeTank[Parallel LC Tank Circuit]
    nodeTank --> nodeFilter[Resonant Frequency Selection]
    nodeFilter --> nodeRfOut[Reconstructed Sine Output]
    nodeVccRF[VCC Supply] --> nodeRfAmp
    nodeRfAmp -.-> nodeHeat[Heat Sink Required for High Power]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Class D PWM Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAudIn[Analog Audio Input] --> nodePWM[PWM Modulator]
    nodePWM --> nodeGate[High Speed Switch Transistor]
    nodeVccD[DC Supply] --> nodeGate
    nodeGate --> nodeLCFilt[LC Low Pass Filter]
    nodeLCFilt --> nodeSpeak[Speaker Load]
    nodeSpeak --> nodeAudOut[High Efficiency Audio Output]
```

### 4.5 Efficiency vs Conduction Angle (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart[Conduction Angle 0 Deg] --> nodeMid1[Angle Increasing]
    nodeMid1 --> nodeMid2[180 Deg Class B]
    nodeMid2 --> nodeMid3[Between 180 and 360 Class AB]
    nodeMid3 --> nodeEnd[360 Deg Class A]
    nodeStart --- nodeEffNote[Approaches 100 Percent Efficiency]
    nodeEnd --- nodeEffNote2[Only 25 to 50 Percent Efficiency]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define a power amplifier. How is it classified based on the conduction angle?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A power amplifier is a large-signal amplifier that delivers appreciable power to a low-impedance load such as a speaker or antenna. It is classified based on the **conduction angle (θ)** of the output transistor over one complete cycle of the input signal. The major classes are: **Class A (θ = 360°)**, **Class B (θ = 180°)**, **Class AB (180° < θ < 360°)**, **Class C (θ < 180°)**, and **Class D (switching mode)**. The conduction angle is directly controlled by the Q-point biasing of the transistor.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — classification basis; 1 mark — list of classes)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is crossover distortion? In which class of power amplifier is it observed, and how is it eliminated?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Crossover distortion is the **non-linear flat region** that appears in the output waveform of a Class B push-pull amplifier near the zero-crossing point. It occurs because each transistor requires a minimum base-emitter voltage (~0.6 V for silicon) before it begins to conduct, creating a "dead zone" of approximately **2 V_γ** in the transfer characteristic. It is observed in **Class B** amplifiers. It is eliminated by using **Class AB biasing**, where both transistors are pre-biased at a small quiescent current (using diodes or a V_BE multiplier) so they remain on the verge of conduction at zero crossing.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — class; 1 mark — elimination method)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its conversion efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A transformer-coupled Class A amplifier uses a step-down audio transformer at the output to match the low-impedance speaker to the high-impedance collector of the transistor. The Q-point is set at the center of the *transformer-reflected* load line using a bias network (typically a voltage divider with emitter resistor). The transformer's primary winding acts as the collector load (DCR ≈ 0), so the entire V_CC appears as V_CEQ, allowing the full V_CC swing.

**Derivation of Efficiency (5 marks):**

*Step 1 — DC power input:*

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

*Step 2 — AC power delivered to load (through transformer):*

For a sinusoidal output current $i_c = I_p \sin(\omega t)$ and load $R_L$ (reflected through turns ratio $n$ as $R_L' = n^2 R_L$):

$$P_{ac} = \frac{I_p^2 \cdot R_L'}{2} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L'}$$

*Step 3 — For maximum symmetrical swing, $I_p = I_{CQ}$ and $V_p = V_{CC}$:*

$$P_{DC, \text{max}} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} \quad ; \quad P_{ac, \text{max}} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$$

*Step 4 — Efficiency:*

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\%}$$

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the operation of a **complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull power amplifier** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive its maximum conversion efficiency and the maximum power dissipation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull stage uses an **NPN transistor (Q1)** and a **PNP transistor (Q2)** connected in series between the $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ rails, with the load $R_L$ connected between their common emitter point and ground. Both transistors are biased at cut-off ($V_{BE} = 0$). During the positive half cycle of input, Q1 conducts and Q2 is OFF; during the negative half cycle, Q2 conducts and Q1 is OFF. Each transistor conducts for 180° (half cycle).

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency (3 marks):**

*DC power input (with peak output voltage $V_p$):*

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}$$

*AC power delivered to load:*

$$P_{ac} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$$

*Efficiency:*

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\pi \cdot V_p}{4 V_{CC}}$$

*Maximum when $V_p = V_{CC}$:*

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$$

**Maximum Power Dissipation (2 marks):**

Setting $\frac{dP_D}{dV_p} = 0$ where $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac}$:

$$V_{p, \text{peak}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

$$P_{D, \text{max}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx \frac{0.405 \cdot V_{CC}^2}{R_L}$$

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q.B (a)** With a neat circuit diagram and transfer characteristics, explain the working of a **Class AB power amplifier**. Compare it with Class A and Class B in terms of efficiency and distortion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit and Working (3 marks):**
A Class AB push-pull amplifier uses two complementary BJTs (or MOSFETs) biased such that the Q-point is **slightly above cut-off**. This is achieved using a **diode bias network** (D1, D2) or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier between the bases, providing a small forward bias of approximately $1.2\text{–}1.4\text{ V}$ across both base-emitter junctions. As a result, both transistors conduct a small quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ even at zero input signal. When the input is positive, Q1 conducts more heavily; when negative, Q2 conducts more. The "dead zone" of Class B is eliminated because both transistors are *already on the edge* of conduction.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class AB |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Q-point | Center | Cut-off | Slightly above cut-off |
| Conduction angle | 360° | 180° | 180°–360° |
| Max efficiency | 25% (series) / 50% (XFMR) | 78.5% | 50%–78.5% |
| Crossover distortion | None | YES | **None** (eliminated) |
| Thermal stability | Poor (high quiescent current) | Good | Good (with bias compensation) |
| Application | Pre-amp | Output (compromised) | **Hi-Fi output** |

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the operation of a **Class C tuned amplifier** with a circuit diagram. Why is a tuned (LC tank) load mandatory? Derive an expression for its efficiency in terms of conduction angle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Operation and Circuit (3 marks):**
A Class C amplifier is biased with the base-emitter junction **reverse-biased**, so the Q-point lies below cut-off. The transistor conducts only when the input signal exceeds the cut-in voltage, producing narrow current pulses at the collector (conduction angle < 180°). The collector is connected to a **parallel LC tank circuit** (L and C in parallel) tuned to the input signal frequency.

**Why the Tuned Load is Mandatory (2 marks):**
The narrow current pulses have high harmonic content. The LC tank, being a **band-pass filter at resonance**, rings at the fundamental frequency (acting as a flywheel), suppressing harmonics and reconstructing a near-sinusoidal output. Without the tank, the output would be highly distorted and unusable. The high-Q tank also prevents the supply voltage from being shorted to ground by the transistor.

**Efficiency in terms of Conduction Angle θ (2 marks):**

For a half-sinusoidal current pulse of conduction angle θ, the peak fundamental component of the output voltage is:

$$V_p = \frac{I_{DC} \cdot R_L \cdot (\theta - \sin\theta)}{1 - \cos(\theta/2)}$$

The efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\pi \cdot (\theta - \sin\theta)}{2 \theta \cdot [1 - \cos(\theta/2)]}$$

As $\theta \to 0$, $\eta \to 100\%$; at $\theta = 180°$, $\eta = 78.5\%$ (degenerates to Class B).

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the Q-point location with the signal swing** — The Q-point is the *DC* operating point, not the *AC* swing. Drawing the Q-point at V_CC/2 in a Class B circuit is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in push-pull DC power** — The supply current is the *sum* of currents drawn from BOTH $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ rails. Many students compute $I_{DC}$ for one transistor only and lose a mark.
> 3. **Maximum dissipation at $V_p = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, NOT at $V_p = V_{CC}$** — This is a **classic trick question** in KTU. Maximum heat dissipation occurs at *less than* full output, not at full output.
> 4. **Class C without a tank circuit** — A Class C amplifier without a tuned LC load is **functionally invalid**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the tank is missing from the diagram.
> 5. **Mixing up Class AB and Class A definitions** — Class AB has conduction angle *between* 180° and 360°, not exactly 360° (that would be Class A).
> 6. **Units and rounding** — Always quote efficiency as a percentage (78.5%, not 0.785). KTU strictly checks units.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Classification is based on the conduction angle θ** of the output transistor over one cycle (360°).
- ✅ **Class A**: θ = 360°, Q at center, η_max = 25% (series) or 50% (transformer-coupled).
- ✅ **Class B**: θ = 180°, push-pull, η_max = 78.5% (= π/4), has crossover distortion.
- ✅ **Class AB**: 180° < θ < 360°, Q just above cut-off, eliminates crossover distortion — most common in Hi-Fi audio.
- ✅ **Class C**: θ < 180°, Q below cut-off, requires a **tuned LC tank** at output, η → 100% theoretically, used in RF amplifiers.
- ✅ **Class D**: Switching (PWM) mode, η up to 100%, requires LC low-pass filter at output.
- ✅ **Maximum power dissipation in Class B** occurs at $V_p = 2V_{CC}/\pi$ with $P_{D,max} = 2V_{CC}^2 / (\pi^2 R_L) \approx 0.405 V_{CC}^2 / R_L$.
- ✅ **Crossover distortion** = dead zone in Class B output near zero crossing; eliminated by **Class AB biasing** (diode bias or V_BE multiplier).
- ✅ **The fundamental trade-off**: Efficiency ↔ Linearity (you cannot maximize both).
- ✅ **For KTU derivations**, memorize the Class B efficiency derivation — it is the **most frequently asked** long-answer derivation in Module 3.
- ✅ Always state the Q-point location **before** stating the conduction angle in any class description.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class A and Class B Power Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Class A and Class B Power Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Amplifier?

A **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal amplifier designed to deliver substantial power to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, or motor) with minimum distortion and maximum possible efficiency. Unlike small-signal voltage amplifiers that focus on voltage gain, the primary goals of a power amplifier are:

1. **Maximum Power Output** to the load
2. **Maximum Efficiency** (ratio of AC power delivered to load vs. DC power drawn from supply)
3. **Minimum Distortion** (linearity of output signal)
4. **Maximum Power Dissipation Capability** of the transistor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A power amplifier is an electronic amplifier designed to increase the magnitude of **power** of a given input signal, as opposed to a voltage or current amplifier. It must be capable of driving low-impedance loads and is typically the final stage in an amplifier chain.

### 1.2 Classification of Power Amplifiers (Conduction Angle)

Power amplifiers are classified based on the **conduction angle (θ)** of the output transistor — that is, the portion of the input signal cycle during which the transistor conducts.

| Class | Conduction Angle | Q-Point Location | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | 360° (full cycle) | Center of load line | Hi-fi audio, low power |
| **Class B** | 180° (half cycle) | At cutoff | Push-pull audio, radio |
| **Class AB** | 180° – 360° | Between cutoff and center | Most common audio amplifiers |
| **Class C** | < 180° | Below cutoff | RF tuned amplifiers, oscillators |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 3, the focus is strictly on **Class A** and **Class B** amplifiers.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚴

> **"Imagine a water pump delivering water to a sprinkler:"**
>
> - **Class A:** The pump runs **continuously at full pressure**, even when the sprinkler is off. Lots of energy is wasted as heat. Smooth, clean output but very inefficient (like an idling car engine burning fuel at a stop).
>
> - **Class B (Push-Pull):** Two pumps work in **alternating shifts** — Pump A sprays for half the rotation, Pump B sprays for the other half. Much less wasted energy, and combined they produce full output. Highly efficient (like hybrid cars that shut off one engine when not needed).
>
> The **push-pull** arrangement of Class B is like a **two-person saw** — one pulls, one pushes, and together they cut faster with less effort than one person doing all the work.

---

## 1.4 The Need for High Efficiency

In small-signal amplifiers, efficiency is rarely a concern because currents are tiny. However, in power amplifiers handling watts to kilowatts, even a small loss appears as significant heat. The theoretical maximum efficiencies are:

- **Class A (Series-fed):** 25%
- **Class A (Transformer-coupled):** 50%
- **Class B (Push-Pull):** **78.5%** ($\pi/4$)

> [!TIP]
> The product of **efficiency × power output** = power dissipated as heat. Higher efficiency means smaller heat sinks, longer battery life, and lower operating costs — critical in portable and high-power applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class A Power Amplifier

### 2.1.1 Series-Fed Class A Amplifier

In a series-fed configuration, the load $R_L$ (or collector resistor $R_C$) is connected directly in series with the collector and the supply $V_{CC}$.

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

- The Q-point is biased at the **midpoint of the DC load line** so that maximum symmetrical swing is possible without clipping.
- The transistor **conducts for the full 360°** of the input cycle.
- The DC operating current $I_{CQ}$ flows continuously regardless of input signal.
- The DC voltage across the transistor is approximately $V_{CC}/2$, leaving $V_{CC}/2$ as the maximum peak AC swing.

**Why is efficiency low?**
- The transistor always draws $I_{CQ}$ from $V_{CC}$ — even when no signal is present, full DC power $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ is consumed.
- Only a fraction of this DC power converts to useful AC output.

---

### 2.1.2 Transformer-Coupled Class A Amplifier

The series-fed amplifier is inefficient because $V_{CE}$ at Q-point is $V_{CC}/2$, leaving only half the supply for signal swing. A **transformer-coupled** configuration overcomes this:

- The transformer primary is connected to the collector; the secondary drives the load.
- DC resistance of primary is near zero, so **the entire $V_{CC}$ is available as DC bias** for the transistor.
- The Q-point sits at $(V_{CC}, I_{CQ})$ — top of the load line.
- AC load is reflected through the transformer turns ratio $n = N_1/N_2$, giving $R_L' = n^2 R_L$.
- Maximum AC swing is now $V_m = V_{CC}$ and $I_m = I_{CQ}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why transformer coupling improves efficiency:** The DC voltage drop across the transformer primary is essentially zero (only copper loss), so the full $V_{CC}$ is across the transistor. The AC load line is steeper than the DC load line, allowing full voltage swing $V_{CC}$ at the collector.

---

## 2.2 Class B Power Amplifier (Push-Pull Configuration)

### 2.2.1 Why Push-Pull?

A single Class B transistor only conducts for **half** of the input cycle (180°), producing severe distortion. To reconstruct the full waveform, **two matched transistors** operate in a push-pull arrangement:

- **Q1 (NPN):** Conducts during the **positive** half-cycle.
- **Q2 (PNP):** Conducts during the **negative** half-cycle.
- The two halves are combined at the output (load) to form a complete waveform.

### 2.2.2 Operating Principle

- Q-point is set exactly at **cutoff** ($I_{BQ} = 0$, $I_{CQ} \approx 0$).
- Each transistor conducts for exactly 180° — a half-wave.
- No DC current flows when there is no input → theoretically **zero quiescent power dissipation**.
- The average DC current drawn is $I_{DC} = I_m/\pi$ (where $I_m$ is peak signal current).

### 2.2.3 Crossover Distortion ⚠️

A critical problem in Class B push-pull amplifiers is **crossover distortion**:

- For input voltages between 0 and $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$, neither transistor conducts.
- The output has a "dead zone" near the zero-crossing, distorting low-amplitude signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution:** Use **Class AB** biasing — apply a small forward bias ($V_{BE} \approx 0.6\text{ V}$) using diodes or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier to keep both transistors slightly ON at idle, eliminating the dead zone. KTU Module 3 introduces this as the practical improvement.

### 2.2.4 Complementary Symmetry Push-Pull

Uses a complementary pair (NPN + PNP) driven by the same input signal. The transformer is eliminated entirely — the load is connected directly between the output node and ground.

- **Advantage:** No bulky, expensive, frequency-limiting output transformer.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires matched complementary transistors; output swing limited to $\pm V_{CC}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** All the formulas below are **must-memorize** for KTU ESE. Memorize the symbols, conditions, and limits — not just the equations.

| # | Parameter | Class A (Series-Fed) | Class A (Transformer-Coupled) | Class B (Push-Pull) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC Power Input, $P_{DC}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_{CC} \cdot I_m}{\pi}$ |
| 2 | AC Power Output, $P_{out}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{4}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2}$ |
| 3 | Maximum $V_m$ | $V_{CC}/2$ | $V_{CC}$ | $V_{CC}$ |
| 4 | Maximum $I_m$ | $I_{CQ}$ | $I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC}/R_L$ |
| 5 | Power Dissipated, $P_D$ | $P_{DC} - P_{out}$ | $P_{DC} - P_{out}$ | $P_{DC} - P_{out}$ |
| 6 | Maximum Efficiency, $\eta_{max}$ | **25%** | **50%** | **$\pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$** |
| 7 | Conduction Angle | 360° | 360° | 180° per transistor |
| 8 | Q-point | Center of load line | Center of AC load line | At cutoff |
| 9 | Harmonic Distortion | Lowest | Low | Moderate (crossover) |
| 10 | Quiescent Current | $I_{CQ}$ (full) | $I_{CQ}$ (full) | $\approx 0$ |

### 2.3.1 Universal Power Conversion Efficiency Formula

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%}$$

### 2.3.2 Figure of Merit (KTU 2019 Pattern)

The **Figure of Merit** of a power amplifier is the ratio of maximum AC output power to the maximum power dissipation rating of the transistor:

$$\text{FoM} = \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{D(max)}}$$

- For Class A: FoM ≈ 0.5 (only half the dissipation capability is usable)
- For Class B: FoM ≈ **5** (one transistor handles full cycle, but the figure uses one device rating)

### 2.3.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Public address (PA) systems | Class B / AB | Efficiency + moderate fidelity |
| Hi-Fi home audio | Class A | Best linearity, lowest distortion |
| AM/FM radio output | Class B | Narrow bandwidth, high efficiency |
| RF transmitter finals | Class C | Highest efficiency, narrowband |
| Hearing aids / portable | Class D (switching) | Battery life, >90% efficiency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Efficiency of Series-Fed Class A Amplifier

### Step 1 — DC Power Supplied

The DC supply $V_{CC}$ delivers a constant current $I_{CQ}$ (the quiescent collector current, unchanged by signal). Therefore:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum AC Output Power

The transistor can swing $V_{CE}$ from $V_{CC}/2$ (Q-point) up to $V_{CC}$ and down to 0. Thus, the maximum peak AC voltage amplitude is:

$$V_{m(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

Similarly, the maximum peak current swing is $I_{m(max)} = I_{CQ}$.

The maximum AC power delivered to the load is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{out(max)} &= \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} \\
&= \frac{\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}\right) \cdot I_{CQ}}{2} \\
&= \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Efficiency

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{4}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}} \\
&= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max,\, \text{Series-Fed Class A}} = 25\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** 75% of the DC power is **always** wasted as heat, even at full output. This is why series-fed Class A is rarely used for high-power applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency of Transformer-Coupled Class A Amplifier

### Step 1 — DC Power

The DC current $I_{CQ}$ flows from $V_{CC}$ through the transformer primary (no DC voltage drop):

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum AC Output

Now the transistor can swing from $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ (Q-point) down to 0. Maximum peak amplitude is $V_m = V_{CC}$. With reflected load $R_L' = n^2 R_L$:

$$V_{m(max)} = V_{CC}, \quad I_{m(max)} = I_{CQ}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{out(max)} &= \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} \\
&= \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Efficiency

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}} \\
\boxed{\eta_{max,\, \text{Transformer Class A}} &= 50\%}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Why 50% and not 100%?** Even with full output, the DC supply is delivering constant $I_{CQ}$. The AC swing can extract at most half the DC power — the other half is dissipated as heat because the average collector voltage remains at $V_{CC}/2$ over a full cycle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier ⭐ (Most Important for KTU)

### Step 1 — Instantaneous Output Current and Voltage

Let the input be a sinusoid. Each transistor conducts for half the cycle, so the output current is a half-wave sinusoid:

$$i_c(t) = \begin{cases} I_m \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0 & \text{for } \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

And similarly the output voltage across the load $R_L$:

$$v_o(t) = i_c(t) \cdot R_L = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(for the active half)}$$

where $V_m = I_m \cdot R_L$.

### Step 2 — Average DC Current from Supply

The DC supply sees the **average** of the half-wave current (because each transistor draws from $V_{CC}$ only during its half). For a full-wave combination of two half-waves (one from each transistor), the average is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{DC} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} i_c(t) \, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ I_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_0^{\pi} + I_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi}^{2\pi} \right] \\
&= \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[ (1 - (-1)) + (-1 - 1) \right] \\
\text{Wait, let me redo this carefully for push-pull...}
\end{aligned}$$

For a **push-pull** stage, BOTH transistors feed into the same $V_{CC}$. The total current drawn from $V_{CC}$ is the sum of the two half-waves, which is a **full-wave rectified sinusoid**:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{DC} &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_0^{\pi} \\
&= \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[ -(-1) - (-(1)) \right] \\
&= \frac{I_m}{\pi} (2) = \frac{2 I_m}{\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

So the **DC power** from the supply is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{DC} &= V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} \\
&= V_{CC} \cdot \frac{2 I_m}{\pi} \\
&= \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Why 2 in the numerator?** Because both transistors alternately draw current — the supply sees the **full-wave average** ($2 I_m / \pi$), not the half-wave average ($I_m / \pi$).

### Step 3 — AC Power Delivered to Load

The output is a full sinusoid (combined push-pull output) with peak amplitude $V_m = I_m R_L$:

$$P_{out} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Maximum Efficiency (at $V_m = V_{CC}$)

Substituting $I_m = V_m / R_L$:

$$P_{out} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}, \quad P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}}{\dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}} \\
&= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC}^2} \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max,\, \text{Class B Push-Pull}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%}$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Power Dissipation in Class B

Power dissipated in transistors = $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out}$

To find where $P_D$ is maximum, differentiate with respect to $V_m$ and set to zero:

$$P_D = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_m}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

$$\frac{dP_D}{dV_m} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m}{R_L} = 0 \implies V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

Substituting back:

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \right)^2 = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$

> **Critical Note for KTU:** The maximum transistor dissipation occurs at $V_m = 2 V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636 V_{CC}$, **NOT at full output** ($V_m = V_{CC}$). This is a common exam trap!

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Class A transformer-coupled power amplifier operates from $V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$ with quiescent current $I_{CQ} = 100\text{ mA}$. The load is $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$ reflected to the primary as $R_L' = 200\text{ }\Omega$. Find $P_{DC}$, $P_{out(max)}$, and $\eta_{max}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** DC Power
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2\text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum AC Power
$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{20 \times 0.1}{2} = 1\text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum Efficiency
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

**Step 4:** Power Dissipated at Q-point (no signal)
$$P_{D(no\,signal)} = P_{DC} = 2\text{ W (all as heat!)}$$

> This is why Class A amplifiers get very hot even when idle.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Class B Push-Pull

**Problem:** A Class B push-pull amplifier uses $V_{CC} = 25\text{ V}$ and $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$. Find $P_{out(max)}$, $P_{DC(max)}$, $\eta_{max}$, and $P_{D(max)}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Maximum peak current
$$I_m = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L} = \frac{25}{8} = 3.125\text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum output power
$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{25 \times 3.125}{2} = 39.06\text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** DC power at full output
$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 25 \times 3.125}{\pi} = 49.74\text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum efficiency
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$$

**Step 5:** Maximum power dissipation in transistors
$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \frac{625}{9.87 \times 8} = 7.92\text{ W}$$

**Verification:** $P_{DC} = P_{out} + P_D \Rightarrow 49.74 \approx 39.06 + 7.92 = 46.98\text{ W}$ ✓ (small numerical rounding)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Class A vs Class B — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASS_A ["CLASS A AMPLIFIER - Conduction 360 degrees"]
        direction LR
        IN_A["Input Signal\nSinusoidal"] --> Q1A["Q1 NPN\nAlways ON"]
        Q1A --> OUT_A["Output Load\nR_L"]
        VCC_A["V_CC Supply"] -.-> Q1A
        QPT_A["Q-point at CENTER\nI_CQ flows always"] -.-> Q1A
    end

    subgraph CLASS_B ["CLASS B PUSH-PULL - Conduction 180 degrees per device"]
        direction LR
        IN_B["Input Signal\nSinusoidal"] --> SPLIT["Phase Splitter\nor complementary drive"]
        SPLIT --> Q1B["Q1 NPN\nConducts POSITIVE half"]
        SPLIT --> Q2B["Q2 PNP\nConducts NEGATIVE half"]
        Q1B --> MERGE["Common Output Node"]
        Q2B --> MERGE
        MERGE --> OUT_B["Output Load\nR_L or Speaker"]
        VCC_B["Plus Minus V_CC"] -.-> Q1B
        VCC_B -.-> Q2B
        QPT_B["Q-point at CUTOFF\nI_CQ equals zero"] -.-> Q1B
    end

    EFF_A["Efficiency 25 to 50 percent\nLow distortion"] --> CLASS_A
    EFF_B["Efficiency 78.5 percent\nCrossover distortion risk"] --> CLASS_B
```
