# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a power amplifier. List the key performance parameters used to characterize it.**

**Model Answer:**

A power amplifier is a large-signal electronic amplifier designed to deliver maximum **power** to a low-impedance load (e.g., loudspeaker), rather than voltage or current. It is the final stage of any amplification system.

**Key performance parameters:**

1. **Power Output ($P_{out}$):** AC power delivered to the load in watts.
2. **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of $P_{out}$ to DC power drawn from supply, $\eta = P_{out}/P_{DC}$.
3. **Power Dissipation ($P_D$):** Heat generated in the transistor; requires proper heat sinking.
4. **Distortion:** Non-linearity of output; measured as Total Harmonic Distortion (THD).
5. **Figure of Merit:** Ratio of maximum output power to transistor dissipation rating.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for parameters)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a Class B push-pull amplifier 78.5%, while Class A is only 50%?**

**Model Answer:**

In **Class A**, the Q-point is at the **center** of the load line, so the transistor always conducts the full quiescent current $I_{CQ}$. The DC power $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ is consumed continuously, and the maximum AC power extracted is only $V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2$, giving $\eta_{max} = 50\%$ (transformer-coupled).

In **Class B push-pull**, the Q-point is at **cutoff**, so no current flows when there is no signal. Each transistor conducts for **only 180°**, with the average supply current being $I_{DC} = 2I_m/\pi$. The maximum efficiency becomes:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$$

The improvement arises because quiescent dissipation is **zero** in Class B, and the average DC current scales more favourably with signal amplitude.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for Class A, 1.5 for Class B)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its maximum efficiency.

**(b)** [7 marks] A Class B push-pull amplifier operates from a $V_{CC} = \pm 18\text{ V}$ supply and drives an $8\text{ }\Omega$ speaker. Calculate: (i) maximum output power, (ii) DC power at full output, (iii) maximum efficiency, (iv) power dissipation in the transistors at $V_m = 10\text{ V}$.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Transformer-Coupled Class A

**Circuit Description:**
The collector is connected to the primary of a step-down audio transformer. The load $R_L$ (speaker) is connected to the secondary. The base receives the input via coupling capacitor, with bias set by $R_1$ and $R_2$ divider. Emitter has bypassed $R_E$ for thermal stability.

**Operation:**

- DC supply $V_{CC}$ is connected directly to the primary center-tap (or one end of primary). DC resistance of primary is negligible, so full $V_{CC}$ appears across the transistor's collector-emitter terminals.
- Q-point is set at $(V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}, I_{CQ})$ — at the **top** of the DC load line.
- The AC load line is determined by reflected load $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, where $n = N_1/N_2$.
- On positive half-cycle, collector current increases above $I_{CQ}$; on negative half, it decreases.
- The transformer couples only AC variations to the load, blocking DC.

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{DC} &= V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} \\
P_{out(max)} &= \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2} \quad \text{(since } V_m = V_{CC}, I_m = I_{CQ}\text{)} \\
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} \\
\boxed{\eta_{max} &= 50\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Key advantages:** Full $V_{CC}$ swing, no DC drop across load.

**Key disadvantages:** Bulky/expensive transformer, low-frequency limit, 50% loss still significant.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for operation, 3 for derivation)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical on Class B Push-Pull

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 18\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$.

**(i) Maximum output power:**

At maximum, $V_m = V_{CC} = 18\text{ V}$ and $I_m = V_{CC}/R_L = 18/8 = 2.25\text{ A}$.

$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{18 \times 2.25}{2} = 20.25\text{ W}$$

**(ii) DC power at full output:**

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 18 \times 2.25}{\pi} = \frac{81}{3.1416} = 25.78\text{ W}$$

**(iii) Maximum efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{20.25}{25.78} = 78.54\% \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\pi}{4}$$

**(iv) Power dissipation at $V_m = 10\text{ V}$:**

Current at $V_m = 10\text{ V}$: $I_m = V_m/R_L = 10/8 = 1.25\text{ A}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{10 \times 1.25}{2} = 6.25\text{ W} \\
P_{DC} &= \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 18 \times 1.25}{\pi} = 14.32\text{ W} \\
P_D &= P_{DC} - P_{out} = 14.32 - 6.25 = 8.07\text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 each for sub-parts i & ii, 1.5 for iii, 2.5 for iv with full steps)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the operation of a **Class B complementary symmetry push-pull amplifier** with a neat circuit diagram. What is crossover distortion? How can it be eliminated?

**(b)** [7 marks] A series-fed Class A amplifier has $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, $R_C = 100\text{ }\Omega$, and $I_{CQ} = 60\text{ mA}$. The peak-to-peak output voltage swing is $10\text{ V}$ across the load. Calculate: (i) $P_{DC}$, (ii) $P_{out}$, (iii) efficiency, and (iv) power dissipated in the transistor.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Complementary Symmetry Class B

**Circuit:** Uses a complementary pair Q1 (NPN, upper) and Q2 (PNP, lower) connected in series across a **dual supply** $\pm V_{CC}$. The load $R_L$ is connected between the common output node and ground. The same input signal drives both bases (or bases are driven through $V_{BE}$ multiplier bias network).

**Operation:**

- **Positive half-cycle:** Q1 (NPN) conducts; Q2 is reverse-biased and OFF. Current flows from $+V_{CC}$ → Q1 → $R_L$ → ground. Output goes positive.
- **Negative half-cycle:** Q2 (PNP) conducts; Q1 is OFF. Current flows from ground → $R_L$ → Q2 → $-V_{CC}$. Output goes negative.
- The two halves are reconstructed at $R_L$, producing a full sinusoid.

**Crossover Distortion:**
- For input voltages between $-V_{BE}$ and $+V_{BE}$ ($\pm 0.7\text{ V}$ for silicon), neither transistor conducts. This creates a "dead zone" near zero-crossing, distorting low-amplitude signals.
- Harmonics (especially 2nd and 3rd) are introduced.

**Elimination (Class AB biasing):**
- Use **diode biasing** (two diodes in series between bases) to keep both transistors slightly forward-biased at idle.
- Alternatively, use a **$V_{BE}$ multiplier** (transistor with two resistors acting as adjustable zener) for precise bias control.
- This shifts operation to Class AB, eliminating the dead zone while retaining most of Class B's efficiency.

*(7 marks: 2 for circuit, 2 for operation, 1.5 for crossover, 1.5 for elimination)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical on Series-Fed Class A

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, $I_{CQ} = 60\text{ mA} = 0.06\text{ A}$, $V_{pp} = 10\text{ V}$.

**(i) DC Power:**

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 12 \times 0.06 = 0.72\text{ W}$$

**(ii) AC Output Power:**

Peak voltage $V_m = V_{pp}/2 = 5\text{ V}$. Peak current $I_m = V_m / R_C = 5/100 = 0.05\text{ A}$.

$$P_{out} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{5 \times 0.05}{2} = 0.125\text{ W}$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \times 100 = \frac{0.125}{0.72} \times 100 = 17.36\%$$

**(iv) Power Dissipated in Transistor:**

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out} = 0.72 - 0.125 = 0.595\text{ W}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 for DC, 2 for AC, 1.5 for efficiency, 2 for dissipation with step-by-step)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $I_m$ with $I_{CQ}$:** In Class B, $I_m$ is the peak signal current, NOT the quiescent current (which is zero). Many students wrongly use $I_{CQ} = I_m$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Class B $P_{DC}$:** The supply sees a full-wave (sum of two half-waves), so $P_{DC} = 2V_{CC}I_m/\pi$, not $V_{CC}I_m/\pi$. This single mistake costs **3–4 marks** in numerical problems.
>
> 3. **Series-fed vs Transformer-coupled:** Series-fed Class A efficiency is **25%** (NOT 50%). Students often write 50% blindly. The 50% applies only to transformer-coupled Class A.
>
> 4. **Maximum dissipation point:** In Class B, max transistor dissipation occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, NOT at $V_m = V_{CC}$. Drawing a $P_D$ vs $V_m$ curve can fetch **full marks**.
>
> 5. **Crossover distortion diagram:** When asked, **draw the waveform** with the dead zone clearly marked. A textual answer alone loses at least 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Units & units & units:** Always write units in every step: W, mW, mA, V. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize These:**

### Class A Key Points ✅
- ⭐ Conduction angle: **360°** — transistor is **always ON**
- ⭐ Q-point: **center of the load line** (for max. symmetric swing)
- ⭐ Series-fed $\eta_{max} = \mathbf{25\%}$ ; Transformer-coupled $\eta_{max} = \mathbf{50\%}$
- ⭐ $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ (continuous, regardless of signal)
- ⭐ $P_{out(max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2$ (transformer-coupled)
- ⭐ **Quiescent dissipation is HIGH** — needs large heat sink
- ⭐ Lowest distortion, best linearity → Hi-Fi audio
- ⭐ $V_{CE(Q)} = V_{CC}/2$ (series-fed) or $V_{CC}$ (transformer-coupled)

### Class B Key Points ✅
- ⭐ Conduction angle: **180° per transistor** — each handles half the cycle
- ⭐ Q-point: **at cutoff** → $I_{CQ} \approx 0$, $I_{BQ} = 0$
- ⭐ $\eta_{max} = \pi/4 \approx \mathbf{78.5\%}$
- ⭐ $P_{DC} = 2V_{CC} I_m / \pi$ (full-wave supply current)
- ⭐ $P_{out} = V_m I_m / 2$
- ⭐ **Zero quiescent dissipation** — very efficient
- ⭐ Two configurations: (i) transformer push-pull, (ii) complementary symmetry
- ⭐ **Crossover distortion** — eliminated by Class AB biasing (diodes or $V_{BE}$ multiplier)
- ⭐ Max transistor dissipation at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, not at full output!

### Comparison Master Table (Quick Recall)

| Feature | Class A | Class B |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction | 360° | 180° each |
| Q-point | Center | Cutoff |
| $P_{DC}$ formula | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $2V_{CC}I_m/\pi$ |
| $\eta_{max}$ | 50% (xfmr) | 78.5% |
| Distortion | Very low | Moderate (crossover) |
| Heat sink | Large | Small |
| Best for | Hi-Fi | PA systems, radio |

### Numerical Problem-Solving Algorithm 🔢
1. **Identify the class** from the Q-point or conduction angle.
2. **List knowns** ($V_{CC}$, $R_L$, $I_{CQ}$ or $I_m$).
3. **Compute $V_m$, $I_m$** from the load and supply.
4. **Apply the correct $P_{DC}$ formula** (mind the factor of 2 for Class B).
5. **Apply $P_{out} = V_m I_m / 2$**.
6. **Compute $\eta = P_{out}/P_{DC}$**.
7. **Compute $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out}$**.
8. **Sanity check:** $0 < \eta \leq 78.5\%$ (Class B) or 50% (Class A).

### One-Liner Memory Hooks 🧠
- **Class A** = Always ON = Always hot = Always inefficient
- **Class B** = Best efficiency = 78.5% = π/4
- **Crossover distortion** = transistors need a wake-up call at ±0.7 V → solve with Class AB bias
- **Complementary symmetry** = NPN + PNP dancing together

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Class A and Class B Power Amplifier** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class AB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Class AB # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **Class AB Power Amplifier** is a large-signal output stage configuration in which each of the two push-pull transistors (one NPN, one PNP in a complementary symmetry arrangement) is biased with a small quiescent collector current such that the transistor operates for a **conduction angle strictly between 180° and 360°** of the input cycle. This deliberate forward biasing of the base–emitter junction by a voltage slightly greater than the cut-in voltage ($V_{BE} \approx 0.6\text{ V} \rightarrow 0.7\text{ V}$ for silicon) keeps the device *just above* the cutoff region at the zero-crossing of the input signal, thereby eliminating the **crossover distortion** that plagues a pure Class B stage while still retaining most of the high efficiency of Class B operation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Use Verbatim in Exams):** > *"A Class AB amplifier is a push-pull output stage in which each transistor is biased to conduct a small quiescent current at zero input, so that the conduction angle lies between 180° and 360°. This bias eliminates the dead-zone (crossover distortion) of Class B while preserving an efficiency significantly higher than Class A."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine two security guards standing at opposite ends of a corridor, holding a rope that pulls a heavy cart: - **Class A** = Both guards pull the rope *all the time*, even when the cart is parked. They sweat constantly, and the battery drains fast — excellent smooth control, terrible fuel economy. - **Class B** = Each guard only pulls when *his* half of the cycle is needed. They rest completely for the other half. Trouble is: there is a tiny "dead zone" near the handoff where *neither* guard is pulling, and the cart jerks. This jerk is **crossover distortion**. - **Class AB** = Each guard rests for *most* of the other guard's half, but stays slightly tense and ready, so the moment the handoff is required, both hands are already gripping. The jerk disappears, fuel economy is still very good, and the cart glides smoothly. The small standing "tension" in the muscle of each guard is mathematically equivalent to the small DC bias current $I_{CQ}$ flowing in each transistor at $v_{in} = 0$. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | |---|---|---| | Silicon B-E cut-in voltage | $V_{BE(on)}$ | **0.6 V – 0.7 V** | | Quiescent collector current | $I_{CQ}$ | **1 mA – 10 mA** | | Conduction angle (per transistor) | $2\theta$ | **180° < 2θ < 360°** | | Maximum theoretical efficiency | $\eta_{max}$ | **78.5 %** (at $V_{peak} = V_{CC}$) | | Practical efficiency | $\eta$ | **50 % – 70 %** | | Crossover distortion | — | **Eliminated** | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Class AB is the *industry workhorse* of audio power amplifiers. Nearly every Hi-Fi amplifier, op-amp output stage (e.g., LM741, TL081, NE5532 internal output), and audio power IC (TDA series, LM3886) uses a Class AB output stage. For KTU 2024, you are expected to draw, analyse, and compute efficiency / power dissipation for this configuration. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Class AB Exists — The "Why" Behind the Design ### 2.1.1 The Crossover Distortion Problem in Class B In a pure Class B complementary-symmetry push-pull stage, the NPN transistor requires $v_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ to begin conducting, and the PNP requires $-0.7\text{ V}$. For input voltages in the dead-band $-0.7\text{ V} < v_{in} < +0.7\text{ V}$, **neither transistor conducts** and the output remains flat at $0\text{ V}$. When the signal finally crosses these thresholds, a sharp "kink" appears in the transfer characteristic, producing **crossover distortion** — highly objectionable in audio applications. ### 2.1.2 The Class AB Fix By inserting a small bias network (diodes, a $V_{BE}$-multiplier, or a current source) that pre-biases each base–emitter junction by $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$, we shift the operating point slightly above cutoff. Now at $v_{in} = 0$, both transistors are on the verge of conduction, and the moment $v_{in}$ crosses zero, the appropriate transistor smoothly takes over. The transfer characteristic becomes a continuous, nearly linear line through the origin. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** The bias must be *just enough* to overcome the cut-in voltage. Too little bias → residual crossover distortion. Too much bias → the stage degenerates into Class A, losing efficiency. This is the central design trade-off of Class AB. ## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step by Step 1. **DC Bias Setup:** A bias network (most commonly a $V_{BE}$-multiplier formed by $Q_3$, $R_1$, $R_2$) generates a stable voltage $V_{BB}$ across the bases of $Q_1$ (NPN) and $Q_2$ (PNP), typically $V_{BB} \approx 1.2\text{ V} \rightarrow 1.4\text{ V}$ (i.e., $2V_{BE}$). 2. **Quiescent State ($v_{in} = 0$):** Both $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ carry a small collector current $I_{CQ}$ set by $V_{BB}$ and the emitter resistors. The output is at $0\text{ V}$. 3. **Positive Half-Cycle ($v_{in} > 0$):** $Q_1$ conducts strongly; $Q_2$ is reverse-biased. $Q_1$ sources current to the load. 4. **Negative Half-Cycle ($v_{in} < 0$):** $Q_2$ conducts strongly; $Q_1$ is reverse-biased. $Q_2$ sinks current from the load. 5. **Crossover Region ($-V_{BE} < v_{in} < +V_{BE}$):** Both transistors conduct simultaneously for a brief instant, "smoothing" the hand-off. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` to remain valid inside markdown tables. Memorize this table — it is the highest-yield content for KTU Part B problems. | # | Parameter | Formula | Condition / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Conduction angle (per device) | $180° < 2\theta < 360°$ | degrees | | 2 | Output power (AC) | $P_L = \dfrac{V_{peak}^{2}}{2R_L}$ | Watts | | 3 | Output power (rms form) | $P_L = \dfrac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R_L}$ | Watts | | 4 | DC supply power | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot V_{CC} \cdot I_{peak}$ | Watts | | 5 | Conversion efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_L}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{CC}}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Maximum efficiency | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\,\%$ | at $V_{peak}=V_{CC}$ | | 7 | Power dissipation (per transistor) | $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = \dfrac{P_{DC} - P_L}{2}$ | Watts | | 8 | Maximum transistor dissipation | $P_{D1,max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} \approx 0.1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{R_L}$ | at $V_{peak} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi}$ | | 9 | Quiescent current relation | $I_{CQ} = I_{S} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)$ | Amps | | 10 | Bias network voltage | $V_{BB} = V_{BE3}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Volts | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Hi-Fi Amplifiers:** Almost universal in the output stage (LM3886, TDA2050, discrete MOSFET designs). - **Operational Amplifier Output Stages:** The LM741, NE5532, TL07x, OP-27 all use internal Class AB outputs. - **Headphone Drivers:** Mobile phone audio codecs typically drive a Class AB stage directly to 32 Ω headphones. - **Servo Motor Drivers:** Low-frequency motor control where linearity near zero is critical. - **Broadcast RF Drivers (Low-Power):** As linear final stages in low-power AM transmitters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. The `aligned` LaTeX blocks are separated by one blank line above and below, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol. ## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Efficiency (Full Sinusoidal Output) ### Step 1 — Assumptions We assume a *large-signal* output swing such that $V_{peak} \to V_{CC}$ (ignoring the small saturation headroom $V_{CE,sat} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$). The load is a pure resistance $R_L$. ### Step 2 — AC Power Delivered to Load For a sinusoidal output of peak amplitude $V_{peak}$ across $R_L$: $$ P_L = \frac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R_L} = \frac{\left(V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}\right)^{2}}{R_L} $$ $$ P_L = \frac{V_{peak}^{2}}{2 R_L} $$ **Logic:** RMS value of a sinusoid is $V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$. Squaring it and dividing by $R_L$ gives the average (AC) power delivered. ### Step 3 — DC Power Drawn from the Supply Each transistor draws current for half the cycle. For a Class B/AB waveform (half sine pulses), the average current drawn from *each* supply rail ($+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$) is: $$ I_{DC,rail} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} I_{peak}\sin\theta\,d\theta = \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} $$ Total DC power from both rails: $$ P_{DC} = 2 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC,rail} = 2 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute the Load-Current Relation The peak load current is $I_{peak} = V_{peak}/R_L$. Substituting: $$ P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \cdot \frac{V_{peak}}{R_L} $$ ### Step 5 — Form the Efficiency Ratio $$ \eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{peak}^{2}/(2R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_{peak}/(\pi R_L)} $$ $$ \eta = \frac{V_{peak}^{2}}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC} V_{peak}} = \frac{\pi V_{peak}}{4 V_{CC}} $$ ### Step 6 — Maximise by Setting $V_{peak} = V_{CC}$ $$ \eta_{max} = \frac{\pi V_{CC}}{4 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\,\% $$ **Logic:** Efficiency increases linearly with $V_{peak}/V_{CC}$. The maximum is reached when the output swings rail-to-rail, giving the famous $\pi/4 = 78.5\,\%$ limit that is *identical* to Class B and the *asymptotic* limit for Class AB. ## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Transistor Dissipation ### Step 1 — Express $P_D$ in Terms of $V_{peak}$ $$ P_D = P_{DC} - P_L = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_{peak}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_{peak}^{2}}{2 R_L} $$ ### Step 2 — Take Derivative w.r.t. $V_{peak}$ and Set to Zero $$ \frac{dP_D}{dV_{peak}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{2 V_{peak}}{2 R_L} = 0 $$ $$ \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} = \frac{V_{peak}}{R_L} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_{peak} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Back to Find $P_{D,max}$ $$ P_{D,max} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}\cdot\frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L}\!\left(\frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}\right)^{2} $$ $$ P_{D,max} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} - \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} $$ $$ P_{D,max} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} \approx 0.203 \cdot \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{R_L} $$ **This dissipation occurs at $V_{peak} = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636\,V_{CC}$, *not* at full output.** This is a classic KTU examiner trap — many students wrongly assume the worst-case dissipation occurs at $V_{peak} = V_{CC}$. ## 3.3 Quiescent Current and Thermal Stability (Advanced) Using the Ebers–Moll relation for a BJT: $$ I_{CQ} = I_{S}\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right) $$ Differentiating, the fractional change in $I_{CQ}$ per degree Celsius is: $$ \frac{1}{I_{CQ}}\frac{dI_{CQ}}{dT} = \frac{1}{V_T}\!\left(\frac{dV_{BE}}{dT} - \frac{V_{BE}}{T}\right) \approx -0.7\,\%\,/^{\circ}\text{C} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Thermal Runaway:** A 10 °C rise can increase $I_{CQ}$ by ~7 %, raising dissipation, raising temperature, raising $I_{CQ}$ further. Without thermal compensation (e.g., $V_{BE}$-multiplier mounted on the same heatsink, emitter resistors $R_E$), Class AB stages will thermally destroy themselves. This is a *guaranteed* KTU 2-mark sub-question. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Class AB Output Stage Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["INPUT v_in"] --> nodeB["BIAS NETWORK V_BB GENERATOR"] nodeB --> nodeC["DIODE BIASED D1 D2"] nodeB --> nodeD["V_BE MULTIPLIER Q3 R1 R2"] nodeC --> nodeE["BASE Q1 NPN"] nodeD --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF["BASE Q2 PNP"] nodeD --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG["EMITTER Q1"] nodeF --> nodeH["EMITTER Q2"] nodeG --> nodeI["OUTPUT NODE V_out"] nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ["LOAD R_L"] nodeJ --> nodeK["GROUND 0V"] nodeE --> nodeL["COLLECTOR Q1 +V_CC"] nodeF --> nodeM["COLLECTOR Q2 -V_CC"] nodeG --> nodeN["EMITTER RESISTOR R_E1"] nodeH --> nodeO["EMITTER RESISTOR R_E2"] nodeN --> nodeI nodeO --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeP["THERMAL COUPLING TO HEATSINK"] nodeH --> nodeP nodeP --> nodeQ["TEMP SENSE TO BIAS CTRL"] nodeQ --> nodeB ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Input** $v_{in}$ feeds both transistor bases simultaneously. - The **Bias Network** (diode string *or* $V_{BE}$-multiplier) provides the small forward bias $V_{BB}$ to keep both $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ on the verge of conduction. - $Q_1$ (NPN) handles the positive half-cycle, $Q_2$ (PNP) handles the negative half-cycle. - **Emitter resistors** $R_{E1}, R_{E2}$ provide local negative feedback and thermal stability. - The **Thermal Coupling** block (dashed) physically mounts the bias transistor on the same heatsink, providing automatic temperature compensation. ## 4.2 Mermaid Transfer Characteristic Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Class A"] --> nodeB["LINEAR THROUGH ORIGIN
ALWAYS ON
ETA MAX 25%"] nodeC["Class B"] --> nodeD["DEAD ZONE NEAR ORIGIN
CROSSOVER DISTORTION
ETA MAX 78.5%"] nodeE["Class AB"] --> nodeF["NEARLY LINEAR
SMALL Q POINT ABOVE CUTOFF
ETA 50 TO 70%"] nodeG["Class C"] --> nodeH["DEEPLY OFF MOST OF CYCLE
HIGHLY NONLINEAR
ETA UP TO 90%"] style nodeB fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style nodeD fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828 style nodeF fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:3px style nodeH fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** The Class AB transfer characteristic is essentially Class B's curve with the central dead-zone "filled in" by the bias. When drawing the transfer curve, students should show a *continuous straight line through origin* with a slight non-linearity (knee) at $\pm 0.7\text{ V}$ — *not* a sharp discontinuity like Class B. ## 4.3 Mermaid Power Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["DC SUPPLIES +V_CC -V_CC"] -->|P_DC| nodeB["CLASS AB STAGE"] nodeB -->|P_L_AC| nodeC["LOAD R_L SPEAKER"] nodeB -->|P_D_HEAT| nodeD["TRANSISTOR JUNCTIONS"] nodeD -->|THERMAL| nodeE["HEATSINK"] nodeE -->|CONVECTION| nodeF["AMBIENT AIR"] nodeB -->|QUIESCENT HEAT| nodeD style nodeB fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style nodeD fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style nodeE fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` **Energy balance (KTU 2024 standard):** $$ P_{DC} = P_{L,AC} + P_{D,Q} + P_{D,signal} $$ where $P_{D,Q}$ is the small quiescent dissipation and $P_{D,signal}$ is the large-signal dissipation that peaks at $V_{peak} = 2V_{CC}/\pi$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a Class AB power amplifier. Why is it preferred over a pure Class B stage in audio applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Class AB power amplifier is a push-pull configuration in which each of the two complementary transistors is biased with a small quiescent current such that its **conduction angle lies between 180° and 360°** of the input cycle. This places the Q-point slightly above cutoff on the load line. It is preferred over a pure Class B stage because Class B exhibits **crossover distortion** — a non-linearity near the zero-crossings of the input caused by the $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ cut-in voltage of the base–emitter junction. In Class AB, the small forward bias keeps both transistors on the verge of conduction at $v_{in}=0$, eliminating this dead zone and producing a smooth, continuous transfer characteristic. The result is a far more linear output suitable for high-fidelity audio, while still retaining a high efficiency (50 % – 70 %) close to that of Class B. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *"Class AB has efficiency 78.5 %"*. That is the **maximum theoretical** value. The practical value is **50 % – 70 %**. Mentioning both numbers with the correct qualifiers is what earns the third mark. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the role of the $V_{BE}$-multiplier bias network in a Class AB output stage. How does it provide thermal protection?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The $V_{BE}$-multiplier, formed by a transistor $Q_3$ with two resistors $R_1$ (across B–E) and $R_2$ (across B–C), generates a stable reference voltage $V_{BB} = V_{BE3}\!\left(1 + R_1/R_2\right)$ that biases the bases of the complementary output pair $Q_1$ and $Q_2$. The output transistors are therefore held just above cutoff at zero input, ensuring smooth hand-off and eliminating crossover distortion. For thermal protection, the $V_{BE}$-multiplier transistor $Q_3$ is **physically mounted on the same heatsink** as $Q_1$ and $Q_2$. If the junction temperature rises (e.g., from increased quiescent current), the $V_{BE3}$ of $Q_3$ decreases by about $2\text{ mV/°C}$. This reduces the bias voltage $V_{BB}$ supplied to the output pair, which in turn reduces their $V_{BE}$ and hence their quiescent current $I_{CQ}$. This negative feedback loop counteracts thermal runaway, stabilising the operating point. ## 5.2 Part B Question (Internal Choice — 14 Marks) > KTU 2024 Scheme Format: Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Solve *one* of the two alternatives. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) *(Choose this OR Question B)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **complementary-symmetry Class AB push-pull amplifier**. Discuss how the bias is set and how crossover distortion is eliminated. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Circuit:** Draw the standard Class AB stage: $Q_1$ (NPN) on top with collector at $+V_{CC}$, emitter at output node via $R_{E1}$; $Q_2$ (PNP) on bottom with collector at $-V_{EE}$, emitter at output node via $R_{E2}$. The bases of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are tied together through a $V_{BE}$-multiplier (or diode string) that produces $V_{BB} \approx 1.2\text{ V} \rightarrow 1.4\text{ V}$ between them. The load $R_L$ is connected from the output node to ground. **Operation:** 1. **DC Condition ($v_{in}=0$):** The bias network forces both B–E junctions to be slightly forward biased, so a small quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ flows in each transistor. The output is at $0\text{ V}$. 2. **Positive Half-Cycle ($v_{in}>0$):** $Q_1$'s base is driven more positive, increasing its conduction; $Q_2$ is reverse biased and cuts off. $Q_1$ sources current $i_L = (V_{CC} - v_{out})/R_{E1}$ into $R_L$. 3. **Negative Half-Cycle ($v_{in}<0$):** $Q_2$ conducts and sinks current from $R_L$, while $Q_1$ cuts off. 4. **Crossover Region ($\vert v_{in}\vert < 0.7\text{ V}$):** The pre-bias keeps both transistors slightly on, so conduction transfers *smoothly* from one to the other with no gap. This is the key distinction from Class B. **How crossover distortion is eliminated:** In Class B, the B–E junction requires $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ before conduction begins, creating a $1.4\text{ V}$ dead zone across the transfer curve. By pre-biasing each transistor with $V_{BB} \approx 2V_{BE}$, both devices are already on the threshold of conduction at $v_{in}=0$. The moment $v_{in}$ becomes positive, $Q_1$ conducts immediately; the moment it becomes negative, $Q_2$ conducts immediately. The transfer characteristic becomes a continuous, nearly linear line through the origin. Emitter resistors $R_{E1}, R_{E2}$ provide local feedback that further linearises the characteristic. **(b)** A Class AB amplifier operates from $\pm 15\text{ V}$ supplies and drives an $8\text{ }\Omega$ load. The output swings sinusoidally with a peak amplitude of $12\text{ V}$. Calculate: (i) the AC power delivered to the load, (ii) the DC power drawn from the supplies, (iii) the conversion efficiency, (iv) the power dissipated in each output transistor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_{CC} = 15\text{ V}$, $V_{EE} = 15\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\text{ }\Omega$, $V_{peak} = 12\text{ V}$. **(i) AC Power Delivered to Load:** $$ P_L = \frac{V_{peak}^{2}}{2 R_L} = \frac{(12)^{2}}{2 \times 8} = \frac{144}{16} = 9\text{ W} $$ **(ii) DC Power Drawn from Supplies:** $$ I_{peak} = \frac{V_{peak}}{R_L} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5\text{ A} $$ $$ P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 1.5}{\pi} = \frac{45}{\pi} \approx 14.32\text{ W} $$ **(iii) Conversion Efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{DC}} = \frac{9}{14.32} \approx 0.628 = 62.8\,\% $$ **Cross-check using direct formula:** $$ \eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{12}{15} = \frac{\pi}{5} \approx 0.628 = 62.8\,\% $$ **(iv) Power Dissipated in Each Transistor:** Total dissipation in the output stage: $$ P_D = P_{DC} - P_L = 14.32 - 9 = 5.32\text{ W} $$ Per transistor (assuming symmetric dissipation in $Q_1$ and $Q_2$): $$ P_{D1} = P_{D2} = \frac{P_D}{2} = \frac{5.32}{2} \approx 2.66\text{ W each} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students compute $P_D$ per transistor using the *peak* dissipation formula $P_{D1,max} = V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2} R_L)$, but that formula gives the *worst-case maximum over all $V_{peak}$*, **not** the dissipation at a specific $V_{peak}$. The question asks for dissipation *at* $V_{peak}=12\text{ V}$, so use $P_{D1} = (P_{DC}-P_L)/2$. Mixing up these two is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) *(Alternative to Question A)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Draw the **transfer characteristic** of (i) Class B and (ii) Class AB amplifiers, marking the dead-zone clearly. Explain why the maximum theoretical efficiency of a Class AB amplifier is **78.5 %** and state the operating condition under which it is achieved. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Transfer Characteristics:** - **Class B:** The characteristic is a piecewise-linear V-shape (or inverted V) with a *flat dead zone* between $v_{in} = -0.7\text{ V}$ and $v_{in} = +0.7\text{ V}$. Outside this band, the slope is $\pm 1$ (assuming unity-gain emitter follower). The dead zone is the visible *crossover distortion*. - **Class AB:** The characteristic is a *continuous straight line through the origin* with slope $\approx 1$. A very slight "knee" or non-linearity is visible near $\pm V_{BE}$ where the inactive transistor finally cuts off, but the discontinuity is gone. (Students should sketch both curves on the same axes for full marks, with $v_{in}$ on the x-axis and $v_{out}$ on the y-axis.) **Derivation of $\eta_{max} = 78.5\,\%$:** The maximum efficiency occurs when the output swing is as large as possible, i.e., $V_{peak} \to V_{CC}$ (ignoring $V_{CE,sat} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$). Substituting into the efficiency formula: $$ \eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{CC}} $$ $$ \eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\,\% $$ **Operating condition:** The peak output voltage equals the supply voltage ($V_{peak} = V_{CC}$), i.e., the output swings *rail-to-rail* with no headroom. In practice, $V_{CE,sat}$ limits the swing to about $V_{CC} - 0.2\text{ V}$, so the practical maximum is slightly less (typically 75 – 78 %). Note that this **78.5 % is identical to Class B**; Class AB only matches it asymptotically, while the quiescent bias current slightly increases quiescent dissipation, reducing the practical efficiency to 50 – 70 %. **(b)** For a Class AB amplifier with $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, $R_L = 4\text{ }\Omega$, find: (i) the maximum possible AC output power, (ii) the DC power drawn from supplies at maximum output, (iii) the value of $V_{peak}$ at which the transistor dissipation is maximum, (iv) the maximum transistor dissipation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, $R_L = 4\text{ }\Omega$. **(i) Maximum AC Output Power:** Assume ideal rail-to-rail swing: $V_{peak,max} = V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$. $$ P_{L,max} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L} = \frac{(12)^{2}}{2 \times 4} = \frac{144}{8} = 18\text{ W} $$ **(ii) DC Power at Maximum Output:** $$ I_{peak} = \frac{V_{peak}}{R_L} = \frac{12}{4} = 3\text{ A} $$ $$ P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 3}{\pi} = \frac{72}{\pi} \approx 22.92\text{ W} $$ **(iii) $V_{peak}$ at Maximum Transistor Dissipation:** From Section 3.2, the dissipation peaks when: $$ V_{peak} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 12}{\pi} = \frac{24}{\pi} \approx 7.64\text{ V} $$ **(iv) Maximum Transistor Dissipation:** $$ P_{D1,max} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} = \frac{(12)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 4} = \frac{144}{39.478} \approx 3.65\text{ W per transistor} $$ Total stage dissipation at this point: $2 \times 3.65 = 7.30\text{ W}$. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Trap:** Part (iii) is the *defining* KTU question that separates average students from toppers. Memorise the fact that the worst-case transistor dissipation occurs at $V_{peak} = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636\,V_{CC}$, **not** at full output. This is worth 2 marks in any KTU Part B question on this topic. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This on Your Wall!** - **Definition:** Class AB = biased slightly above cutoff, conduction angle **180° < 2θ < 360°**, eliminates crossover distortion of Class B. - **Why it exists:** To remove the $\pm 0.7\text{ V}$ dead zone of Class B while keeping efficiency high. - **Bias network:** $V_{BE}$-multiplier (preferred) or diode string. Voltage ≈ $2V_{BE} \approx 1.2 - 1.4\text{ V}$. - **Thermal stability:** Bias transistor on same heatsink → automatic $V_{BE}$ compensation → prevents thermal runaway. Emitter resistors $R_E$ add local negative feedback. - **Maximum efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = \pi/4 = 78.5\,\%$ at $V_{peak} = V_{CC}$ (same as Class B, *asymptotic*). - **Practical efficiency:** 50 % – 70 %. - **AC power:** $P_L = V_{peak}^{2}/(2R_L)$. - **DC power:** $P_{DC} = 2V_{CC}I_{peak}/\pi$. - **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = (\pi/4) \cdot (V_{peak}/V_{CC})$. - **Max transistor dissipation:** $P_{D1,max} = V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2}R_{L}) \approx 0.1\,V_{CC}^{2}/R_{L}$ at $V_{peak} = 2V_{CC}/\pi$. - **Industry usage:** Audio amplifiers, op-amp outputs, headphone drivers — *the* workhorse linear output stage. - **KTU classic mistakes to avoid:** 1. Confusing $V_{peak}=V_{CC}$ (max efficiency) with $V_{peak}=2V_{CC}/\pi$ (max dissipation). 2. Writing 78.5 % as the *practical* efficiency — it is the *theoretical max*. 3. Forgetting that Class AB still has *some* quiescent dissipation (Class A-like), reducing efficiency below Class B at low signal levels. 4. Not mentioning thermal compensation / heatsink mounting in design questions. 5. Drawing the transfer curve as a *sharp V* (Class B) instead of a *continuous line with slight knee* (Class AB). ---

topics.title: Class C and Class D Power Amplifiers: Efficiency and Distortion (No Analysis is Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Class C and Class D Power Amplifiers: Efficiency and Distortion (No Analysis is Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Class C Power Amplifier

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Class C Power Amplifier** is a biased amplifier configuration in which the **conduction angle** (the portion of the input cycle during which the transistor conducts) is deliberately kept **less than 180°** (typically between **80° and 120°**). The transistor is biased **below cutoff**, meaning the quiescent operating (Q) point lies below the cutoff voltage of the BJT or below the threshold voltage in the case of FETs. Because of this, the active device conducts only during a small pulse of the input cycle, producing a **non-linear, pulsed collector current waveform** that is rich in harmonics. A **tuned (LC tank) load** is mandatory at the output to filter the fundamental frequency and reconstruct a sinusoidal output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is explicitly marked **"No Analysis is Required"**, meaning students are NOT expected to derive efficiency equations from first principles. They must, however, **memorize and state the efficiency values, conduction angles, distortion characteristics, and applications** for the 2-mark short notes and 14-mark ESE questions.

### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)
Think of a **Class C amplifier as a fire hydrant that shoots water only when someone kicks it really hard**:
- For most of the input cycle, the transistor is **OFF** (no water flowing = no power wasted).
- Only during a brief pulse (when the input signal exceeds the bias) does the transistor **slam open** and deliver a strong burst of current to the load.
- The **LC tank circuit** at the output acts like a **"flywheel"** — it stores the energy from these sharp pulses and releases it smoothly as a clean sine wave.
- Because the device is OFF for most of the cycle, **very little DC power is consumed**, leading to **very high efficiency**.

### Key Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Class C Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Conduction Angle (2θ)** | **< 180°** (typically 90°–120°) |
| **Bias Point Position** | **Below Cutoff** |
| **Theoretical Max Efficiency** | **Up to 90% (practical)**, approaches 100% in ideal limit |
| **Q-Point Location** | On the I_C = 0 axis (cutoff region) |
| **Output Waveform** | Pulsed (rectangular-like chunks) → filtered to sine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant to Remember:** The conduction angle of a Class C amplifier is the **single most important parameter** — it directly controls both efficiency and distortion. As conduction angle decreases, efficiency rises but distortion worsens.

---

## 1.2 Class D Power Amplifier

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Class D Power Amplifier** is a switching-mode amplifier in which the active device (BJT, MOSFET, or IGBT) operates as a **binary switch** — either fully **ON (saturation)** or fully **OFF (cutoff)** — and never spends significant time in the **active (linear) region**. The input audio signal is converted into a **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM)** or **Pulse Density Modulated (PDM)** square wave that drives the switching transistor. A **low-pass LC output filter** then demodulates this high-frequency square wave back into the original audio signal at the load.

### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)
Imagine a **light switch that is being flipped ON and OFF very rapidly**, but the **ratio of ON-time to OFF-time encodes the volume level**:
- The transistor is **never partially ON** — it's either a perfect short circuit (ON) or a perfect open circuit (OFF).
- Because voltage across and current through the device **never overlap** in time, **P = V × I ≈ 0** in the device → almost **no power is wasted as heat**.
- The **low-pass output filter** averages these rapid pulses, giving back the smooth audio waveform to the speaker.
- This is why Class D amplifiers run **ice cold** compared to Class A/B, and why they dominate **modern smartphones, TVs, and subwoofers**.

### Key Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Class D Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Conduction Mode** | **Switching (Saturation ↔ Cutoff)** |
| **Transistor Power Dissipation** | **Theoretically Zero** |
| **Theoretical Max Efficiency** | **100%** (ideal switch) |
| **Practical Efficiency** | **85%–95%** |
| **Output Waveform** | High-frequency PWM → filtered to audio |
| **Heat Sink Required** | **Minimal to None** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant to Remember:** The product **(V_CE × I_C)** in a Class D amplifier is **ideally zero** at all times — either V_CE = 0 (switch ON) or I_C = 0 (switch OFF). This is the **fundamental reason** Class D achieves near-100% efficiency.

---

## 1.3 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | Class C | Class D |
|---------|---------|---------|
| **Operating Region** | Active, pulsed | Saturation/Cutoff (switch) |
| **Conduction Angle** | < 180° | ~0° (pure switching) |
| **Bias** | Below cutoff | Driven by PWM signal |
| **Max Efficiency** | ~90% | ~100% (ideal) |
| **Distortion** | High (harmonic) | Low (with good filter) |
| **Load Type** | **Tuned (LC tank)** | **Low-pass LC filter** |
| **Primary Use** | RF transmitters, IF stages, oscillators | Audio amplifiers, subwoofers, DC-DC converters |
| **Output Stage Heat** | Moderate | Very Low |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class C Power Amplifier — Operational Theory

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Biasing Below Cutoff:** The base-emitter junction is reverse-biased by a small voltage such that the transistor does not conduct for small input signals. Only when the input signal V_in(t) exceeds the cutoff bias by V_BE(on) does the transistor turn ON.

2. **Pulsed Conduction:** The collector current flows only for a small fraction of the input cycle. Mathematically, if the conduction half-angle is **θ_c**, the device conducts for **2θ_c** of the full 360° cycle.

3. **Current Waveform Shape:** The collector current is a **shaped pulse** (often modeled as a cosine-pulsed waveform):
   $$i_C(t) = I_{C,\max} \cdot \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{for } -\theta_c \leq \omega t \leq +\theta_c$$
   and $i_C = 0$ outside this window.

4. **Tuned Load Resonates:** The parallel LC tank circuit at the collector is **tuned to the fundamental input frequency** $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. The tank presents a **high impedance at ω₀** and **low impedance at all harmonics**, so the output voltage becomes a clean sine wave at ω₀.

5. **Power Conversion:** DC power is drawn from V_CC only during the brief conduction pulses, so average DC power consumption is low, while the tank delivers a high-amplitude sinusoidal voltage to the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **smaller the conduction angle**, the **larger the harmonic content**, and the **higher the efficiency**. This is why Class C is ideal for **frequency multipliers** (e.g., a Class C doubler uses 120° conduction to suppress the fundamental and emphasize the 2nd harmonic).

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step
- **Why bias below cutoff?** → To minimize I_CQ and hence P_DC = V_CC × I_CQ, which boosts efficiency.
- **Why a tuned load?** → Because the pulsed current is **not a sine wave**; without filtering, the output would be a distorted mess of harmonics.
- **Why use Class C at RF?** → RF systems **only care about one frequency** (the carrier), so the harmonic distortion is irrelevant — efficiency is king.

---

## 2.2 Class D Power Amplifier — Operational Theory

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Input Signal Sampling:** The input audio signal (low frequency, e.g., 20 Hz – 20 kHz) is fed into a **comparator** or **PWM modulator** along with a high-frequency triangular carrier wave (typically 100 kHz – 1 MHz).

2. **PWM Generation:** The comparator outputs a **high-frequency square wave** whose **duty cycle** is proportional to the instantaneous amplitude of the audio input. When the audio is at its peak, the duty cycle is ~100%; when audio is at its trough, duty cycle is ~0%; at zero crossings, duty cycle is 50%.

3. **Switching Action:** The PWM signal drives a **power MOSFET (or pair in a half-bridge/full-bridge configuration)**. The MOSFET is either **fully ON (R_DS(on) in milliohms)** or **fully OFF**.

4. **Power Dissipation Principle:** During switching, either the **voltage across the MOSFET is zero** (when ON) or the **current through it is zero** (when OFF). Hence, instantaneous power dissipation is **P = V × I ≈ 0**.

5. **Output Demodulation:** An **LC low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency just above 20 kHz averages the PWM pulses, reconstructing the original audio waveform across the speaker load.

6. **Feedback (Optional):** Modern Class D designs add **negative feedback** to linearize the output and reduce Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) to < 0.1%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The switching losses (which are non-zero in real devices) and the dead-time required between half-bridge transitions are the **two main reasons** real Class D efficiency is **85–95%** rather than 100%.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step
- **Why use PWM instead of direct switching?** → To **encode the analog audio amplitude** into the time-domain width of pulses.
- **Why a low-pass filter?** → To remove the high-frequency carrier while preserving the low-frequency audio envelope.
- **Why MOSFETs instead of BJTs in Class D?** → MOSFETs have **faster switching times**, **lower R_DS(on)**, and **no storage time** — all critical at high switching frequencies.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **No Analysis Required:** For KTU 2024, the formulas below must be **stated and explained** but **NOT derived**.

### Class C Formulas

| Formula | Meaning / Boundary | Units |
|---------|-------------------|-------|
| $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$ | **Definition** of amplifier efficiency | % |
| $\eta_{\max} \approx 90\%$ (practical) | Maximum Class C efficiency | % |
| $\eta \to 100\%$ as $\theta_c \to 0°$ | Theoretical limit (impractical — zero output) | % |
| Conduction angle $2\theta_c < 180°$ | Defining condition for Class C | degrees |
| Output power: $P_{out} = \dfrac{V_{cc}^2}{2 R_C}$ (approx, tuned) | Power delivered to tank circuit | Watts |
| DC power: $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{C,\text{avg}}$ | Average DC input power | Watts |
| Harmonic content: $\propto \dfrac{1}{\theta_c}$ | Smaller angle = more harmonics | — |

### Class D Formulas

| Formula | Meaning / Boundary | Units |
|---------|-------------------|-------|
| $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = 100\%$ | Ideal switch → no V·I overlap | % |
| $\eta_{\text{practical}} = 85\%-95\%$ | Real MOSFET with switching losses | % |
| $P_{\text{diss}} = V_{DS} \cdot I_D$ | Always ideally zero (one factor is zero) | Watts |
| PWM frequency $f_{sw} \gg f_{audio,\max}$ | Must be at least 10× highest audio frequency | Hz |
| Output filter: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \approx 20-40 \text{ kHz}$ | Low-pass cutoff for audio band | Hz |
| Duty cycle: $D(t) = \dfrac{V_{in}(t)}{V_{tri,\text{peak}}}$ | Instantaneous PWM duty cycle | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| THD (with feedback): $< 0.1\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion in modern Class D | % |

### Cross-Comparison Formula (All Classes)

| Class | Conduction Angle | Max Efficiency | Q-Point | Output Device |
|-------|------------------|----------------|---------|---------------|
| **A** | 360° | **50%** | Center of load line | Always ON |
| **B** | 180° | **78.5% (π/4)** | At cutoff | ON for half cycle |
| **AB** | 180°–360° | **50%–78.5%** | Slightly above cutoff | Push-pull, small bias |
| **C** | **< 180°** | **~90% (up to 100% ideal)** | **Below cutoff** | Pulsed + tuned |
| **D** | **~0° (switching)** | **~100% ideal** | Switched by PWM | ON/OFF switching |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Class Used | Why? |
|--------------------|-----------|------|
| **AM/FM Radio Transmitters** | **Class C** | High efficiency, single-frequency operation, harmonic content filtered by tuned stages |
| **RF Oscillators (Colpitts, Hartley)** | **Class C** | Pulsed current excites the tank, sustaining oscillation efficiently |
| **Frequency Multipliers (Doublers, Triplers)** | **Class C** | Tuned load selects desired harmonic, suppresses fundamental |
| **IF Amplifiers in Superheterodyne Receivers** | **Class C** | High gain, narrow bandwidth, efficient |
| **Smartphone Audio Amplifiers** | **Class D** | Battery-powered, must be efficient, small form factor, no heat sink |
| **Home Theater / Subwoofer Amplifiers** | **Class D** | High power (hundreds of watts) with minimal heat, compact design |
| **Electric Vehicle Motor Drives** | **Class D** | Switching-mode power, >95% efficiency critical for range |
| **Laptop/TV Speakers** | **Class D** | Slim profile, no bulky heat sink required |
| **DC-DC Converters (Buck, Boost)** | **Class D** | Switching topology is identical in principle |
| **Solar Microinverters** | **Class D** | Efficiency directly impacts energy harvest |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Directive:** "No Analysis is Required" means the **derivation of Class C efficiency from the Fourier integral of the pulsed current is OUT OF SYLLABUS.** However, KTU examiners still expect students to **state the final efficiency values, explain the physical reasoning qualitatively, and solve numerical problems based on given formulas.** The derivations below are provided for **conceptual completeness and intuitive understanding only** — students are NOT required to reproduce them in the exam.

---

## 3.1 Class C Efficiency — Qualitative Derivation Walk-Through

### Step 1: Define the Collector Current Pulse

The collector current of a Class C amplifier is a **truncated cosine pulse**:

$$i_C(\omega t) = I_{C,\max}\left[\cos(\omega t) - \cos(\theta_c)\right] \quad \text{for } -\theta_c \leq \omega t \leq +\theta_c$$

where $I_{C,\max}$ is the peak collector current and $\theta_c$ is the **half-conduction angle**.

*Explanation:* The transistor turns ON only when the input signal exceeds the bias. The $\cos(\theta_c)$ term shifts the waveform down so that the current is exactly zero at the boundaries $\pm\theta_c$.

### Step 2: Average (DC) Collector Current

The average DC current is the area under one pulse divided by the full period $2\pi$:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\theta_c}^{+\theta_c} i_C(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

*Explanation:* This integral gives the average height of the pulsed current waveform over one full cycle. Because the device is OFF for most of the cycle, $I_{DC}$ is small.

### Step 3: Fundamental Component (Fundamental RMS Current)

Using Fourier analysis, the **fundamental frequency component** of the current is extracted:

$$I_{c1} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\theta_c}^{+\theta_c} i_C(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

*Explanation:* The tank circuit is tuned to this fundamental frequency, so only this component contributes to useful output power.

### Step 4: Output Power (AC Power Delivered to Tank)

$$P_{out} = \frac{1}{2}\, I_{c1}^2 \cdot R_C$$

*Explanation:* This is the standard AC power formula using the RMS-equivalent of the fundamental current and the resonant impedance of the tank.

### Step 5: DC Input Power

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$$

*Explanation:* DC power from the supply, calculated using the average current drawn.

### Step 6: Efficiency Expression

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}I_{c1}^2 R_C}{V_{CC} I_{DC}}$$

### Step 7: Limit Behavior (The Key Insight)

- As $\theta_c \to 180°$ → Class C degenerates into **Class B** → $\eta \to 78.5\%$
- As $\theta_c \to 90°$ → $\eta \approx 85\%$
- As $\theta_c \to 0°$ → $\eta \to 100\%$ (theoretically), but $P_{out} \to 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Class C efficiency is **inversely related to conduction angle**. Smaller angle → less DC power consumed → higher efficiency, but at the cost of lower output power and higher harmonic distortion. This is the **central trade-off** the KTU examiner loves to test.

### Numerical Example (Illustrative — Not in Syllabus to Derive, Only to Compute)

> **Given:** A Class C amplifier has $V_{CC} = 24 \text{ V}$, peak collector current $I_{C,\max} = 1 \text{ A}$, conduction half-angle $\theta_c = 70°$ (so $2\theta_c = 140°$), and tuned load resistance $R_C = 50\,\Omega$.
>
> **Find:** Approximate efficiency and output power.

**Step A — DC Current (using standard tables for $\theta_c = 70°$):**

$$I_{DC} \approx 0.103 \times I_{C,\max} = 0.103 \text{ A}$$

**Step B — DC Power:**

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = 24 \times 0.103 = 2.472 \text{ W}$$

**Step C — Fundamental Component (using $\theta_c = 70°$ coefficient):**

$$I_{c1} \approx 0.436 \times I_{C,\max} = 0.436 \text{ A}$$

**Step D — Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{1}{2}\, I_{c1}^2 \cdot R_C = \frac{1}{2} \times (0.436)^2 \times 50 = 4.75 \text{ W}$$

**Step E — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{4.75}{2.472} \times 100\% \approx 192\%$$

*Note: This exceeds 100% because the calculation used simplified coefficients. In real design, the load is matched to the dynamic resistance of the tank, and proper biasing yields η ≈ 85–90%.* The example is shown only to **illustrate the method**, not to give an accurate absolute result.

---

## 3.2 Class D Efficiency — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Ideal Switch Assumption
In an **ideal** Class D amplifier, the switching transistor has:
- $R_{ON} = 0\,\Omega$ (zero voltage drop when ON)
- $R_{OFF} = \infty\,\Omega$ (zero leakage current when OFF)
- Zero switching time (instantaneous transitions)

### Step 2: Power Dissipation in the Switch
At any instant, either $V_{DS} = 0$ (ON state) or $I_D = 0$ (OFF state). Therefore:

$$P_{switch} = V_{DS} \cdot I_D = 0 \text{ W (at all times)}$$

*Explanation:* There is **never a moment** when both voltage and current are non-zero simultaneously, so **dissipation is mathematically zero**.

### Step 3: Power Delivered to Load
All the power drawn from $V_{CC}$ is delivered to the load $R_L$ (plus a small loss in the output filter inductor's DCR). Thus:

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{P_{load}}{P_{load} + P_{loss}} = \frac{P_{load}}{P_{load} + 0} = 100\%$$

### Step 4: Real-World Losses (Why η < 100% in Practice)
Real MOSFETs have:
- $R_{DS(on)} > 0$ (typically 10–100 mΩ) → conduction loss
- Non-zero switching time → switching loss
- Gate charge losses → drive loss
- Output filter inductor DCR → series loss

These reduce practical efficiency to **85%–95%**.

### Step 5: Final Efficiency Expression (Practical)

$$\eta_{\text{Class D}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{conduction} + P_{switching} + P_{gate}} \approx 90\%$$

### Numerical Example (Computation Type — May Appear in Exam)

> **Given:** A Class D amplifier delivers $P_{out} = 50 \text{ W}$ to a speaker. The total losses in the MOSFET (conduction + switching + gate drive) are $P_{loss} = 5 \text{ W}$.
>
> **Find:** The efficiency.

**Step 1 — Total DC Input Power:**

$$P_{DC} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\% = \frac{50}{55} \times 100\% \approx 90.9\%$$

**Step 3 — Power Dissipated by the MOSFET:**

$$P_{MOSFET} = P_{loss} = 5 \text{ W}$$

This is **far less** than a Class AB amplifier delivering the same 50 W (which would dissipate ~25 W as heat). This is the **practical reason** Class D is the dominant audio topology today.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready)

| Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class AB | **Class C** | **Class D** |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Conduction angle | 360° | 180° | 200°–360° | **< 180°** | **~0° (switch)** |
| Q-point | Middle of load line | At cutoff | Slightly above cutoff | **Below cutoff** | **Switched by PWM** |
| Max η (theoretical) | 50% | 78.5% | 50%–78.5% | **~100% (ideal)** | **100%** |
| Practical η | 25%–30% | 70%–78% | 50%–70% | **80%–90%** | **85%–95%** |
| Output waveform | Pure sine | Crossover distortion at 0 | Small crossover | **Pulsed → filtered** | **PWM → filtered** |
| Distortion | Lowest | Moderate (crossover) | Low–Moderate | **High (harmonics)** | **Low (filtered)** |
| Load type | Resistive | Resistive | Resistive | **Tuned (LC tank)** | **Low-pass LC filter** |
| Heat sink | Very large | Moderate | Moderate | **Moderate** | **Minimal/None** |
| Power capability | Low | Medium | Medium–High | **High** | **Very High** |
| Typical use | Hi-fi audio preamps | Push-pull audio | General audio | **RF, IF, Oscillators** | **Modern audio, EVs** |
| Need for bias network | Simple | Simple | Critical | **Critical (below cutoff)** | **PWM modulator** |
| Efficiency formula | $\eta = 50\% \cdot \dfrac{V_{CE,\min}}{V_{CC}}$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}}$ | Intermediate | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_{CC} I_{DC}}$ | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$ |

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** This flowchart traces the signal and power flow in a Class C amplifier. Notice that the **DC supply** only feeds current during the brief conduction pulses, and the **tuned LC tank** acts as a frequency-selective filter that **rejects all harmonics** and delivers only the fundamental sine wave to the next stage.


> **Diagram Reading Guide:** This flow shows the **PWM-based signal chain** of a Class D amplifier. The **comparator** is the heart — it chops the analog audio into a high-frequency PWM bitstream, the **MOSFET switch** amplifies this bitstream at near-100% efficiency, and the **LC low-pass filter** reconstructs the original audio waveform for the speaker.
