# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Q: Compare Class C and Class D power amplifiers in terms of (i) conduction angle, (ii) maximum theoretical efficiency, and (iii) type of load used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **Class C** | **Class D** |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| (i) Conduction angle | **Less than 180°** (typically 90°–120°) | **Approximately 0°** (pure switching between ON and OFF) |
| (ii) Max theoretical efficiency | **~90% practical, up to 100% ideal** | **100% (ideal switch)** |
| (iii) Load type | **Tuned LC tank circuit** (resonant at fundamental) | **Low-pass LC filter** (reconstructs audio from PWM) |

*Marking scheme: 1 mark for conduction angle, 1 mark for efficiency, 1 mark for load type. Brief explanatory sentence accompanying each point earns full marks.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Q: Why is a tuned (LC tank) circuit essential at the output of a Class C power amplifier? What happens if it is replaced by a purely resistive load?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The collector current of a Class C amplifier is a **pulsed, non-sinusoidal waveform** containing a large DC component plus the fundamental frequency and several harmonics (2f₀, 3f₀, etc.). *(1 mark)*

2. A **tuned LC tank circuit** presents a **high impedance at the resonant frequency f₀ = 1/(2π√LC)** and a **low impedance at all harmonic frequencies**, effectively filtering out the harmonics and delivering a **pure sinusoidal output** at the fundamental frequency. *(1 mark)*

3. If replaced by a **resistive load**, the harmonic components would not be filtered — the output would be a **highly distorted, non-sinusoidal waveform**, and the amplifier would be **useless for communication or amplification purposes**. The harmonic distortion (D) would be extremely high (often >50%). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** split, and students must answer **ONE out of TWO choices**.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the operation of a Class C power amplifier. Discuss its efficiency, distortion characteristics, and typical applications. State why the Q-point is placed below cutoff.**

#### Part (a) — Circuit Operation and Q-Point (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Description:** *(2 marks)*
A Class C power amplifier uses a **BJT (or FET) as the active device**, with the base biased through a **voltage divider and emitter resistor** such that the **V_BE at quiescent conditions is less than the cut-in voltage (V_γ ≈ 0.6 V for silicon)**. The collector is connected to V_CC through a **parallel LC tank circuit** that is tuned to resonate at the input signal frequency f₀. The output is taken across the tank circuit.

**2. Q-Point Location:** *(2 marks)*
The Q-point is deliberately placed **below the cutoff region on the I_C axis** (i.e., I_CQ ≈ 0). This means:
- The transistor is **OFF at the quiescent (no-signal) condition**.
- Conduction occurs **only when the input signal V_in(t) exceeds the cutoff bias by at least V_BE(on) ≈ 0.7 V**.

**3. Pulsed Conduction:** *(2 marks)*
During each cycle of the input signal, the transistor conducts for an angle **2θ_c < 180°** (typically 80°–120°). This produces a **pulsed collector current waveform** containing the fundamental frequency and multiple harmonics. The LC tank **filters out the harmonics** and delivers a **clean sinusoidal output** at f₀.

**4. Sketch (description for the examiner):** *(1 mark)*
```
         V_CC
          |
          |
        [ L ]
          |
   ___    |    ___
  |   |   |   |   |
  | C |---+---|   |--- V_OUT (sine wave at f₀)
  |___|   |   |   |
          |
        [ BJT ]
          |
   ___    |    ___
  | R_E|--+--| R_B2 |
  |___|      |___|
                |
              V_BB (< 0 for NPN below cutoff)
              OR
              V_BB = 0 with proper bias network
```

#### Part (b) — Efficiency, Distortion, and Applications (7 marks)

**1. Efficiency:** *(2 marks)*
Class C amplifiers achieve **theoretical efficiencies of up to 100%** (as conduction angle → 0°), with **practical efficiencies of 80%–90%**. The efficiency formula is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{P_{out}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}}$$

The DC input power is small because $I_{DC}$ is small (device is OFF for most of the cycle). Higher efficiency is obtained by **reducing the conduction angle**, but this also reduces the output power.

**2. Distortion:** *(2 marks)*
Class C amplifiers have **very high harmonic distortion** (D often > 30%–50%) because the pulsed current waveform contains strong harmonics. However, this is **not a problem in RF applications** because:
- The tank circuit **filters out all harmonics**, leaving only the fundamental.
- The system only cares about **one frequency** (the carrier).
- In fact, the harmonics are **used constructively** in **frequency multiplier circuits** (doublers, triplers).

**3. Typical Applications:** *(2 marks)*
- **RF transmitters** (AM broadcast, two-way radios)
- **Intermediate Frequency (IF) amplifiers** in superheterodyne receivers
- **RF oscillators** (Colpitts, Clapp, Crystal oscillators)
- **Frequency multipliers** (doublers, triplers)
- **High-frequency heating** (industrial RF generators)

**4. Why Q-point below cutoff?** *(1 mark)*
The below-cutoff bias ensures **minimum quiescent collector current (I_CQ ≈ 0)**, which minimizes the DC power consumption $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ and **maximizes efficiency**. Without this bias, the amplifier would degenerate into Class B (η ≈ 78.5%) or worse.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> - Confusing **conduction angle** with **phase angle** — they are NOT the same.
> - Drawing the Q-point on the **active region** (which is wrong; it must be on the **cutoff axis**).
> - Forgetting to mention the **tuned LC load** in the circuit diagram (mandatory element!).
> - Stating "Class C has high distortion, so it is bad" — wrong framing. The correct answer is **"the distortion is filtered out by the tank, making it irrelevant in RF applications"**.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Explain the working principle of a Class D power amplifier with a block diagram. Discuss its efficiency, sources of distortion, and state any three real-world applications.**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Block Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Basic Principle:** *(2 marks)*
A Class D power amplifier operates the active device (typically a **power MOSFET**) as a **binary switch** — either fully **ON (saturation)** or fully **OFF (cutoff)**. The analog input signal is first converted into a **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM)** square wave using a high-frequency triangular carrier, then amplified by the switching transistor, and finally **reconstructed into analog** by a low-pass output filter.

**2. Block-by-Block Operation:** *(3 marks)*

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Audio Input** | Low-frequency analog signal (20 Hz – 20 kHz) |
| **Triangular Wave Generator** | Produces a high-frequency carrier wave (typically 250 kHz – 1 MHz) |
| **PWM Comparator** | Compares audio with triangle; outputs HIGH when audio > triangle, LOW otherwise |
| **Gate Driver** | Level-shifts and conditions the PWM signal to drive the MOSFET gate |
| **Power MOSFET (Half/Full Bridge)** | Switches the V_DD supply at the PWM duty cycle to the output |
| **LC Low-Pass Filter** | Filters out the high-frequency PWM carrier, leaving only the audio baseband |
| **Speaker Load** | Receives the reconstructed audio waveform |

**3. Power Dissipation Logic:** *(2 marks)*
The MOSFET never operates in the linear (active) region:
- When **ON**: $V_{DS} \approx 0$ (since $R_{DS(on)}$ is milliohms), so power $P = V_{DS} \times I_D \approx 0$.
- When **OFF**: $I_D = 0$, so power $P = V_{DS} \times 0 = 0$.
- Only during the **brief switching transition** (nanoseconds) is there simultaneous V and I, contributing small switching losses.

#### Part (b) — Efficiency, Distortion, and Applications (7 marks)

**1. Efficiency:** *(2 marks)*
- **Theoretical (ideal switch):** $\eta_{ideal} = 100\%$
- **Practical (real MOSFET):** $\eta_{practical} = 85\%-95\%$

The reduction from 100% is due to:
- Conduction loss: $P_{cond} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$
- Switching loss: $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{rise} + t_{fall}) f_{sw}$
- Gate drive loss: $P_{gate} = Q_g \cdot V_{gs} \cdot f_{sw}$

**2. Sources of Distortion:** *(2 marks)*
- **PWM non-linearity:** Dead-time between half-bridge transitions causes slight distortion (corrected by feedback).
- **Power supply ripple:** Inadequate decoupling injects noise.
- **Output filter non-ideality:** Filter component tolerances affect frequency response.
- **Switching noise:** EMI from high dV/dt edges can couple into the audio path.
- With **negative feedback**, modern Class D amplifiers achieve **THD < 0.1%**, rivaling Class AB quality.

**3. Three Real-World Applications:** *(3 marks — 1 mark each)*
- **(a) Smartphone and tablet audio amplifiers** — battery-powered, compact, cool operation.
- **(b) Subwoofers and home theater systems** — high power (hundreds of watts) with minimal heat.
- **(c) Electric vehicle (EV) audio and motor drive systems** — efficiency directly impacts range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> - Drawing the **block diagram with a BJT in the active region** (wrong — must show MOSFET as a switch with ON/OFF states).
> - **Forgetting the LC low-pass output filter** (this is the key demodulation element).
> - Stating "Class D has 100% efficiency" **without qualifying** that this is the **ideal/theoretical** case; practical is 85–95%.
> - Confusing **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** with **PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)** — they are fundamentally different.
> - Missing the **gate driver** stage in the block diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-minute exam revision!**

### ✅ Class C Power Amplifier — Quick Recall

- [x] **Conduction angle < 180°** (the defining characteristic)
- [x] **Q-point placed BELOW cutoff** (I_CQ ≈ 0)
- [x] **Bias is reverse-biasing** the base-emitter junction at quiescent
- [x] **Output is a pulsed current waveform** rich in harmonics
- [x] **Tuned LC tank load is MANDATORY** — acts as a bandpass filter at fundamental
- [x] **Tank circuit equation:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$
- [x] **Efficiency:** up to **~90% practical**, theoretically approaches 100% as θ_c → 0
- [x] **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_{CC} I_{DC}}$
- [x] **Distortion:** HIGH harmonic distortion, but **filtered out by tank** → irrelevant at RF
- [x] **Smaller conduction angle → higher efficiency but lower output power**
- [x] **Applications:** RF transmitters, IF amplifiers, RF oscillators, frequency multipliers
- [x] **NOT suitable for audio** (broadband) applications

### ✅ Class D Power Amplifier — Quick Recall

- [x] **Transistor operates as a SWITCH** (saturation ↔ cutoff)
- [x] **Conduction angle ≈ 0°** (no linear operation, just ON/OFF)
- [x] **No Q-point in traditional sense** — driven by PWM
- [x] **PWM generation:** comparator of audio vs. high-frequency triangular carrier
- [x] **PWM frequency >> audio frequency** (typically 250 kHz – 1 MHz carrier vs. 20 kHz max audio)
- [x] **Duty cycle** $D(t) = \dfrac{V_{in}(t)}{V_{tri,\text{peak}}}$ — proportional to instantaneous audio
- [x] **LC low-pass output filter is MANDATORY** — demodulates PWM back to audio
- [x] **Efficiency theoretical = 100%** (ideal switch: P = V × I = 0 at all times)
- [x] **Efficiency practical = 85%–95%** (due to conduction, switching, and gate losses)
- [x] **Power dissipation sources:** $R_{DS(on)}$, switching transients, gate charge, filter DCR
- [x] **Distortion:** LOW with proper design (THD < 0.1% with feedback)
- [x] **Sources of distortion:** dead-time, supply ripple, EMI, filter non-ideality
- [x] **Applications:** audio amplifiers, subwoofers, EV motor drives, DC-DC converters, solar inverters
- [x] **Cool operation** → minimal/no heat sink required

### ✅ Cross-Comparison Master Points

- [x] **Class A:** η_max = 50%, conduction 360°, resistive load, low distortion
- [x] **Class B:** η_max = 78.5% (π/4), conduction 180°, push-pull, crossover distortion
- [x] **Class AB:** η between 50–78.5%, small bias to eliminate crossover distortion
- [x] **Class C:** η up to 90%, conduction < 180°, **tuned load**, high harmonic distortion (filtered)
- [x] **Class D:** η up to 100% ideal (85–95% practical), **switching**, **PWM + low-pass filter**

### ✅ Exam-Day Power Phrases (Use these in 14-mark answers for full marks)

- *"The Q-point is deliberately placed below cutoff to minimize the quiescent collector current and thus maximize efficiency."*
- *"The tuned LC tank presents a high impedance at the fundamental and low impedance at harmonics, acting as a bandpass filter."*
- *"In a Class D amplifier, the MOSFET never operates in the active region — it is either fully ON or fully OFF, ensuring that voltage and current never overlap significantly."*
- *"PWM encodes the analog amplitude in the time domain via the duty cycle of a high-frequency square wave."*
- *"The low-pass output filter reconstructs the audio baseband by averaging the high-frequency PWM pulses."*

### ✅ Common KTU Mistakes to AVOID

- ❌ Drawing Class C with Q-point in the active region (it must be **on or below** the I_C = 0 axis)
- ❌ Forgetting the **tuned LC load** in the Class C circuit diagram
- ❌ Claiming Class C "has bad distortion so it is useless" — it is **highly useful in RF**
- ❌ Stating Class D has 100% efficiency without qualifying the **ideal/practical** distinction
- ❌ Confusing Class D with **Class B push-pull** — they are fundamentally different
- ❌ Missing the **PWM modulator stage** in the Class D block diagram
- ❌ Forgetting the **output filter** in the Class D signal chain
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Amplifiers: General Feedback Structure, Properties of Negative Feedback, and Four Basic Feedback Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifiers: General Feedback Structure, Properties of Negative Feedback, and Four Basic Feedback Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Feedback Amplifier

A **Feedback Amplifier** is a two-port electronic network in which a portion of the **output signal** is sampled, processed by a passive **feedback network**, and reintroduced to the **input port** to control and refine the overall amplifier behaviour. Mathematically, feedback is the deliberate coupling of output back to input, which dramatically alters the amplifier's gain, impedance, bandwidth, linearity, and noise performance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, feedback amplifier analysis is treated under **Course Outcome CO3** (Design and analyse amplifier circuits using small-signal models) and is the most heavily weighted concept in Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Feedback Equation (Foundation of Module 3):**
> The **closed-loop gain** of any feedback amplifier is:
> $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$
> where $A$ is the open-loop gain of the basic amplifier, $\beta$ is the feedback factor, and the product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain**. The term $(1 + A\beta)$ is the **desensitivity factor** or **return difference**.

## 1.2 The Two Polarities of Feedback

| Polarity | Effect on Loop Gain | Behaviour | Real-world Risk |
|----------|--------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Negative Feedback (NFB)** | Subtractive: $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ | Stable, controlled, predictable | Slight loss in gain |
| **Positive Feedback (PFB)** | Additive: $A_f = A/(1-A\beta)$ | Oscillation if $A\beta \to 1$ | Unstable, useful only in oscillators |

For the rest of this module, we exclusively deal with **Negative Feedback**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car Steering Wheel on a Hilly Road:**
> Imagine driving a car without a steering wheel. The car accelerates but veers off due to slopes, wind, and bumps (these are *disturbances*). Now, add a steering wheel: every small deviation of the car from the driver's intended path is **sensed (sampled)**, **amplified by your brain**, and **fed back** through your hands to correct the wheels. The path is smoother (lower distortion), more stable (less sensitivity to disturbances), but you must turn the wheel *slightly more* than the actual error (gain reduction). That is **negative feedback in action**. The amplifier is the car engine, the feedback network is the steering linkage, and $(1+A\beta)$ is the correction factor.

A second analogy: a **water-tank level controller**. A float senses the water level (output sampling), compares it to the reference (mixer / summing junction), and drives a valve (basic amplifier). A disturbance like a sudden outflow is automatically corrected.

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The general feedback amplifier has exactly **four functional blocks**:

1. **Basic Amplifier (A)** — provides the raw open-loop gain (active, often unilateral).
2. **Feedback Network (β)** — passive, samples the output and produces a feedback signal.
3. **Summing Junction (Σ)** — subtracts the feedback signal from the source (NFB).
4. **Source and Load Interfaces** — the ports where sampling (output) and mixing (input) take place.

This architecture is universal — it applies equally to op-amps, transistor stages, and integrated circuits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Feedback Structure — Step-by-Step Logic

The classical single-loop feedback model has the following signal flow:

1. **Source** $V_s$ (or $I_s$) drives the input port.
2. **Error Signal** $V_{\epsilon} = V_s - V_f$ is formed at the **summing junction** by subtracting the feedback signal.
3. **Basic Amplifier** multiplies the error: $V_o = A \cdot V_{\epsilon}$.
4. **Feedback Network** samples the output: $V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$.
5. **Closed-loop output** $V_o$ is delivered to the **load**.

The four block transfer relations:
$$V_{\epsilon} = V_s - V_f$$
$$V_o = A \cdot V_{\epsilon}$$
$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$
$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o) = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} V_s$$

This is the **fundamental closed-loop equation**.

## 2.2 The Loop Gain $A\beta$ — Why It Is the Soul of Feedback

The product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain**: the signal magnitude that travels around the loop once (amplifier → feedback network → back to summing junction). It governs *every* feedback property:

- If $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep NFB): $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ — gain is set entirely by the **passive** feedback network.
- If $A\beta = 0$: $A_f = A$ — open-loop behaviour.
- If $A\beta = -1$ (in positive feedback regime): **oscillation** (Barkhausen criterion).

## 2.3 The Four Key Properties of Negative Feedback

### Property 1 — Gain Desensitization
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$
Taking the differential: $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$.
A fractional change in open-loop gain is reduced by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. This is the **desensitization factor**.

### Property 2 — Bandwidth Extension
$$BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$
NFB **widens the bandwidth** by the same factor $(1+A\beta)$ by which it reduces the gain. This is the famous **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) constancy**:
$$A_f \cdot BW_f = A \cdot BW = \text{constant}$$

### Property 3 — Reduction of Nonlinear Distortion
$$D_f = \frac{D}{1 + A\beta}$$
If the open-loop amplifier produces harmonic distortion $D$, the closed-loop distortion is reduced by the desensitization factor, provided the feedback is applied symmetrically across the distortion-generating stage.

### Property 4 — Modification of Input and Output Resistance
Depending on the **sampling type** (series or shunt) at input/output, the impedances are either **increased** or **decreased** by $(1+A\beta)$.

| Sampling at Port | Effect on Resistance | Formula |
|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Series (voltage drop sensed)** | Increases | $R_{if} = R_i (1+A\beta)$ |
| **Shunt (current sensed)** | Decreases | $R_{if} = R_i / (1+A\beta)$ |
| **Voltage sampling (output node)** | Decreases | $R_{of} = R_o / (1+A\beta)$ |
| **Current sampling (output loop)** | Increases | $R_{of} = R_o (1+A\beta)$ |

## 2.4 Classification of the Four Basic Feedback Topologies

The **two sampling methods** (voltage vs current) at the output crossed with the **two mixing methods** (series vs shunt) at the input yield exactly **four canonical topologies**:

| Topology | Output Sampling | Input Mixing | Ideal Transfer Ratio | Ideal $R_i$ | Ideal $R_o$ | Amplifier Type |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Voltage-Series (V-S)** | Voltage | Series | $A_v = V_o/V_s$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | **Voltage Amp** |
| **Voltage-Shunt (V-I)** | Voltage | Shunt | $A_z = V_o/I_s$ | $0$ | $0$ | **Transresistance** |
| **Current-Series (I-S)** | Current | Series | $A_g = I_o/V_s$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | **Transconductance** |
| **Current-Shunt (I-I)** | Current | Shunt | $A_i = I_o/I_s$ | $0$ | $\infty$ | **Current Amp** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling-Mixing Mnemonic for KTU Exams:**
> *Series sampling at input → high $R_{if}$ ; Shunt sampling at input → low $R_{if}$.*
> *Voltage sampling at output → low $R_{of}$ ; Current sampling at output → high $R_{of}$.*

## 2.5 The Ideal Feedback Model — Why We Use It

In the **ideal feedback model** (Sedra-Smith / KTU preferred method):
- The **forward path A** is treated as a **unilateral** voltage-controlled voltage source (or its appropriate dual) with **infinite input resistance** and **zero output resistance**.
- The **feedback network β** is treated as **unilateral** (output → input only) and **does not load** the forward path or load.

In real circuits, this idealization is rarely exact, so we apply **loading corrections** in two stages:
1. **Step A:** Include the feedback network's loading on the input and output loops of the basic amplifier to get the **loaded A**.
2. **Step B:** Find $\beta$ from the feedback network with the input port of the feedback network open-circuited (series) or short-circuited (shunt).

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table: Master Formula Sheet for Feedback Amplifier Analysis**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Condition |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | Universal |
| 2 | Loop gain | $T = A\beta$ | Universal |
| 3 | Desensitization | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}\dfrac{dA}{A}$ | Universal |
| 4 | Bandwidth | $BW_f = BW(1+A\beta)$ | Single-pole approx. |
| 5 | GBP constancy | $A \cdot BW = A_f \cdot BW_f$ | Constant |
| 6 | Distortion | $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1+A\beta}$ | Symmetric feedback |
| 7 | Noise | $N_f = \dfrac{N}{1+A\beta}$ | For noise injected at A |
| 8 | V-S: $R_{if}$ | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ | Series input |
| 9 | V-I: $R_{if}$ | $R_{if} = R_i / (1+A\beta)$ | Shunt input |
| 10 | V-S, V-I: $R_{of}$ | $R_{of} = R_o/(1+A\beta)$ | Voltage sampling |
| 11 | I-S, I-I: $R_{of}$ | $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ | Current sampling |
| 12 | Voltage-Series $A_{vf}$ | $A_{vf} = \dfrac{A_v}{1+A_v\beta}$ | VCVS |
| 13 | Voltage-Shunt $R_{mf}$ | $R_{mf} = \dfrac{R_m}{1+R_m\beta}$ | VCCS-like (shunt-shunt) |
| 14 | Current-Series $G_{mf}$ | $G_{mf} = \dfrac{G_m}{1+G_m\beta}$ | Series-series |
| 15 | Current-Shunt $A_{if}$ | $A_{if} = \dfrac{A_i}{1+A_i\beta}$ | CCCS-like |

**Units of ideal amplifier types (KTU commonly asks):**
- $A_v$ → dimensionless (V/V)
- $R_m$ → **Ohms (Ω)** (V/A)
- $G_m$ → **Siemens (S)** (A/V)
- $A_i$ → dimensionless (A/A)

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-amp design** — the non-inverting and inverting configurations are textbook Voltage-Series and Voltage-Shunt feedback.
- **Audio amplifiers** — NFB reduces total harmonic distortion (THD) from ~1% to <0.001%.
- **RF power amplifiers** — NFB flattens gain across the band.
- **Instrumentation amplifiers** — use current-shunt feedback for precision.
- **Voltage regulators (LM317, 7805)** — all are series-series / shunt-shunt NFB systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Gain from the Basic Loop Equations

We start with the four fundamental signal-flow equations. Each is derived from inspection of the feedback block diagram.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\epsilon} &= V_s - V_f \quad &\text{(Summing junction — NFB subtraction)}\\
V_f &= \beta \cdot V_o \quad &\text{(Feedback network transfer)}\\
V_o &= A \cdot V_{\epsilon} \quad &\text{(Basic amplifier forward transfer)}\\
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute $V_{\epsilon}$ into the third equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= A \cdot (V_s - V_f) \\
    &= A \cdot (V_s - \beta V_o) \\
    &= A \cdot V_s - A \beta \cdot V_o
\end{aligned}
$$

Collect $V_o$ terms on the left:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o + A \beta \cdot V_o &= A \cdot V_s \\
V_o (1 + A\beta) &= A \cdot V_s
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally, isolate the closed-loop gain $A_f = V_o / V_s$:

$$
\boxed{\,A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** When $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep feedback), the denominator dominates and the closed-loop gain collapses to a remarkably simple expression that depends **only on the passive feedback network**:

$$
A_f \;\big\vert_{A\beta \gg 1} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\beta}
$$

This is the **principle of gain stabilization** and is the reason why precision analog circuits (op-amps, INAs) can be designed with high accuracy using only resistors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gain Sensitivity to Open-Loop Variations

We differentiate the closed-loop gain with respect to $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= A \cdot (1 + A\beta)^{-1} \\
\frac{dA_f}{dA} &= (1+A\beta)^{-1} + A \cdot (-1)(1+A\beta)^{-2} \cdot \beta \\
&= \frac{1}{1+A\beta} - \frac{A\beta}{(1+A\beta)^2} \\
&= \frac{(1+A\beta) - A\beta}{(1+A\beta)^2} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide both sides by $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ to obtain the **fractional sensitivity**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dA_f / dA}{A_f} &= \frac{1/(1+A\beta)^2}{A/(1+A\beta)} \\
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} &= \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S_A^{A_f} = \frac{dA_f / A_f}{dA / A} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $A$ changes by 10 % and $1 + A\beta = 100$, then $A_f$ changes by only 0.1 %. The desensitization factor $(1+A\beta)$ acts as a *shock absorber* for parameter drift caused by temperature, ageing, and device spread.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth Extension (Single-Pole Amplifier)

Assume the open-loop amplifier has a single dominant pole at frequency $\omega_H$, so that:
$$A(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega / \omega_H}$$

The 3-dB bandwidth of the open-loop amplifier is $BW = \omega_H$. Substitute into the closed-loop formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f(j\omega) &= \frac{A(j\omega)}{1 + A(j\omega)\beta} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}} \\
&= \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H + A_0 \beta} \\
&= \frac{A_0 / (1 + A_0 \beta)}{1 + \dfrac{j\omega}{\omega_H (1 + A_0 \beta)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recognizing the **new midband gain** $A_{f0} = A_0/(1+A_0\beta)$ and the **new upper cutoff** $\omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1+A_0\beta)$:

$$
\boxed{\,A_{f0} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0 \beta}, \qquad \omega_{Hf} = \omega_H (1 + A_0 \beta)\,}
$$

Multiplying the two:
$$
A_{f0} \cdot \omega_{Hf} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta} \cdot \omega_H(1+A_0\beta) = A_0 \omega_H = \text{GBP}
$$

The **Gain-Bandwidth Product is invariant** under negative feedback. This is the cornerstone of all wideband design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Input Resistance Modification (Series Mixing)

Consider a basic amplifier with input port modelled as $R_i$ in series with a dependent source. The open-loop input current is $i_i = v_{\epsilon} / R_i$. With NFB, the input voltage is $v_i = v_s = v_{\epsilon} + v_f$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{if} &= \frac{v_i}{i_i} = \frac{v_{\epsilon} + v_f}{i_i} = \frac{v_{\epsilon} + A\beta v_{\epsilon}}{i_i} \\
       &= \frac{v_{\epsilon}}{i_i}(1 + A\beta) = R_i(1+A\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

For **shunt mixing** at the input, the input current is the sum $i_i = i_{\epsilon} - i_f$, and the algebra yields:

$$
\boxed{\,R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta} \quad \text{(shunt input)}\,}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Output Resistance Modification (Voltage Sampling)

The open-loop output is modelled as a dependent source $A v_{\epsilon}$ in series with $R_o$. Apply a test voltage $v_t$ at the output with input shorted ($v_s = 0$):

$$
i_t = \frac{v_t - A v_{\epsilon}}{R_o}, \quad v_{\epsilon} = -\beta v_t
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_t &= \frac{v_t - A(-\beta v_t)}{R_o} = \frac{v_t (1 + A\beta)}{R_o} \\
R_{of} &= \frac{v_t}{i_t} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **current sampling**, the dual analysis gives $R_{of} = R_o (1 + A\beta)$.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Effect of Topology on $R_i$ and $R_o$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{if}^{(V\text{-}S)} &= R_i (1+A\beta), \quad R_{of}^{(V\text{-}S)} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} \\
R_{if}^{(V\text{-}I)} &= \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta}, \quad R_{of}^{(V\text{-}I)} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} \\
R_{if}^{(I\text{-}S)} &= R_i (1+A\beta), \quad R_{of}^{(I\text{-}S)} = R_o (1+A\beta) \\
R_{if}^{(I\text{-}I)} &= \frac{R_i}{1+A\beta}, \quad R_{of}^{(I\text{-}I)} = R_o (1+A\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage-Series Feedback** is by far the most common (non-inverting op-amp) because it provides high $R_{if}$ and low $R_{of}$ — the ideal voltage source behaviour.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A basic amplifier has $A = 2000$, $R_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 50\text{ }\Omega$, $\beta = 0.05$. Compute closed-loop gain, input resistance, output resistance, and bandwidth given $BW = 50\text{ kHz}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + A\beta &= 1 + 2000 \times 0.05 = 1 + 100 = 101 \\
A_f &= \frac{2000}{101} = 19.80 \;\text{V/V} \\
R_{if} &= 2\text{ k}\Omega \times 101 = 202\text{ k}\Omega \quad \text{(assuming series input)}\\
R_{of} &= \frac{50}{101} \approx 0.495\;\Omega \quad \text{(assuming voltage sampling)}\\
BW_f &= 50\text{ kHz} \times 101 = 5.05\text{ MHz} \\
\text{GBP} &= A_f \cdot BW_f = 19.80 \times 5.05\text{ MHz} \approx 100\text{ MHz} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Sanity check: $A \cdot BW = 2000 \times 50\text{ kHz} = 100\text{ MHz}$ ✓ (GBP conserved).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Single-Loop Feedback Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Vs] --> sumj[Summing Junction]
    fbNet[Feedback Network beta] -->|Vf| sumj
    sumj -->|V epsilon = Vs - Vf| basicAmp[Basic Amplifier A]
    basicAmp -->|Vo| outNode[Output Vo]
    outNode -->|sampled| load[Load]
    outNode -->|sensed| fbNet
    style src fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style sumj fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#D62728
    style basicAmp fill:#E5F5E0,stroke:#2CA02C
    style fbNet fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style outNode fill:#F0E5F5,stroke:#9467BD
```

The four canonical blocks: source, summing junction, basic amplifier, and feedback network. The output is sampled and fed back to the summing junction where it is subtracted (NFB).

---

## 4.2 Voltage-Series Feedback (Non-Inverting Op-Amp Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input Vs] --> sumjNode[Summing Junction -]
    sumjNode -->|V epsilon| ampA[Basic Amplifier Av]
    ampA -->|Vo| outNode[Output Vo]
    outNode --> loadR[Load RL]
    outNode -->|voltage sample| fbR[Resistor R2]
    inNode --> r1[R1] --> fbNode[Feedback Node]
    fbNode --> fbR
    fbNode -->|Vf series| sumjNode
    style inNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style ampA fill:#E5F5E0,stroke:#2CA02C
    style outNode fill:#F0E5F5,stroke:#9467BD
    style fbR fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

Series input mixing (Vf subtracts in series with Vs); voltage output sampling. $R_{if}$ is high, $R_{of}$ is low. Ideal $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$.

---

## 4.3 Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Inverting Op-Amp Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input Is] --> sumjNode[Summing Junction parallel]
    sumjNode -->|I epsilon| ampA[Basic Amplifier Rm Transresistance]
    ampA -->|Vo| outNode[Output Vo]
    outNode --> loadR[Load RL]
    outNode -->|voltage sample| fbR[Resistor Rf]
    fbR -->|If shunt| sumjNode
    style inNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style ampA fill:#E5F5E0,stroke:#2CA02C
    style outNode fill:#F0E5F5,stroke:#9467BD
    style fbR fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

Shunt input mixing (If is a current summing node); voltage output sampling. $R_{if}$ is low, $R_{of}$ is low. Ideal $\beta = -1/R_f$.

---

## 4.4 Current-Series Feedback (Transconductance Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input Vs] --> sumjNode[Summing Junction -]
    sumjNode -->|V epsilon| ampA[Basic Amplifier Gm Transconductance]
    ampA -->|Io| outLoop[Output Current Loop]
    outLoop --> loadR[Load RL]
    outLoop -->|current sense| fbR[Resistor Rf in series]
    fbR -->|Vf series| sumjNode
    style inNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style ampA fill:#E5F5E0,stroke:#2CA02C
    style outLoop fill:#F0E5F5,stroke:#9467BD
    style fbR fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

Series input mixing; current output sampling (sensed via a series resistor in the output loop). $R_{if}$ is high, $R_{of}$ is high. Ideal $\beta = R_f$.

---

## 4.5 Current-Shunt Feedback (Current Amplifier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input Is] --> sumjNode[Summing Junction parallel]
    sumjNode -->|I epsilon| ampA[Basic Amplifier Ai Current Gain]
    ampA -->|Io| outLoop[Output Current Loop]
    outLoop --> loadR[Load RL]
    outLoop -->|current sense| fbR[Feedback Network]
    fbR -->|If shunt| sumjNode
    style inNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style ampA fill:#E5F5E0,stroke:#2CA02C
    style outLoop fill:#F0E5F5,stroke:#9467BD
    style fbR fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

Shunt input mixing; current output sampling. $R_{if}$ is low, $R_{of}$ is high. Ideal $\beta = I_f/I_o$ from the feedback network.

---

## 4.6 Signal-Flow Graph of Closed-Loop Gain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcN[Vs] --x sumN[+] --> epsN[V epsilon]
    fbN[Vf] --x sumN
    epsN --> gainN[Gain A] --> outN[Vo]
    outN --> betaN[Gain beta] --> fbN
    style srcN fill:#E8F4FD
    style sumN fill:#FFE5E5
    style gainN fill:#E5F5E0
    style betaN fill:#FFF5E0
    style outN fill:#F0E5F5
```

The forward path has gain $A$; the return path (loop) has gain $\beta$. Mason's rule on this single-loop graph gives $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ directly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
> **Q1.** Define negative feedback in amplifiers. Mention any two advantages of applying negative feedback.

**Model Answer:**
Negative feedback is the process by which a portion of the output signal of an amplifier is returned to the input and subtracted from the input signal, thereby reducing the net input to the basic amplifier. Mathematically, the closed-loop gain becomes $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$, where $\beta$ is the feedback factor.
**Two advantages:**
1. **Gain stabilization** — sensitivity of gain to device parameters and temperature is reduced by the factor $(1+A\beta)$.
2. **Bandwidth extension** — the closed-loop bandwidth is increased by the same factor $(1+A\beta)$, keeping the Gain-Bandwidth Product constant.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
> **Q2.** List the four basic feedback topologies and state the ideal input and output resistance for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Topology | Input Mixing | Output Sampling | Ideal $R_i$ | Ideal $R_o$ |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Voltage-Series | Series | Voltage | $\infty$ | $0$ |
| Voltage-Shunt | Shunt | Voltage | $0$ | $0$ |
| Current-Series | Series | Current | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| Current-Shunt | Shunt | Current | $0$ | $\infty$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier. Show that negative feedback reduces the sensitivity of gain to variations in open-loop gain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An amplifier has open-loop gain $A = 1000$, input resistance $R_i = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, output resistance $R_o = 100\text{ }\Omega$, and bandwidth $BW = 100\text{ kHz}$. A feedback factor $\beta = 0.1$ is applied in a voltage-series configuration. Find the closed-loop gain $A_{vf}$, input resistance $R_{if}$, output resistance $R_{of}$, and closed-loop bandwidth $BW_f$. Verify the constancy of Gain-Bandwidth Product. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the four basic feedback topologies. State the type of amplifier realized by each and the corresponding expression for closed-loop gain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A voltage-series feedback amplifier uses a basic amplifier of gain $A_v = 500$ and feedback network with $\beta = 0.04$. If the open-loop input resistance is $R_i = 4\text{ k}\Omega$ and output resistance is $R_o = 80\text{ }\Omega$, calculate the closed-loop voltage gain, input resistance, output resistance, and the reduction in gain. Also comment on the effect on bandwidth if the open-loop $BW = 200\text{ kHz}$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

### Solution to A(a) — Closed-Loop Gain and Sensitivity Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the four functional blocks — source $V_s$, summing junction, basic amplifier of gain $A$, and feedback network of transfer ratio $\beta$.

**Step 2:** Write the summing junction equation (negative feedback):
$$V_{\epsilon} = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 3:** Write the feedback network equation:
$$V_f = \beta V_o$$

**Step 4:** Write the basic amplifier equation:
$$V_o = A V_{\epsilon}$$

**Step 5:** Combine the three equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= A(V_s - V_f) = A(V_s - \beta V_o) \\
V_o + A\beta V_o &= A V_s \\
V_o(1 + A\beta) &= A V_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Closed-loop gain:
$$\boxed{\,A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}\,}$$

**Step 7:** Differentiate with respect to $A$:
$$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$

**Step 8:** Compute fractional sensitivity:
$$
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{dA_f/dA}{A_f} = \frac{1/(1+A\beta)^2}{A/(1+A\beta)} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}
$$

**Conclusion:** A fractional change in $A$ produces only a fraction $1/(1+A\beta)$ of that change in $A_f$. This is the desensitization property of negative feedback.

### Solution to A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $A = 1000$, $R_i = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 100\text{ }\Omega$, $BW = 100\text{ kHz}$, $\beta = 0.1$, voltage-series configuration.

**Step 1:** Compute the desensitization factor:
$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 1000 \times 0.1 = 1 + 100 = 101$$

**Step 2:** Closed-loop gain:
$$A_{vf} = \frac{1000}{101} = 9.90 \;\text{V/V}$$

**Step 3:** Input resistance (series mixing → increase):
$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta) = 1\text{ k}\Omega \times 101 = 101\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Output resistance (voltage sampling → decrease):
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.990\;\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Bandwidth (extension by $(1+A\beta)$):
$$BW_f = BW (1 + A\beta) = 100\text{ kHz} \times 101 = 10.1\text{ MHz}$$

**Step 6:** Verify GBP:
- Open-loop GBP: $A \cdot BW = 1000 \times 100\text{ kHz} = 100\text{ MHz}$
- Closed-loop GBP: $A_{vf} \cdot BW_f = 9.90 \times 10.1\text{ MHz} \approx 100\text{ MHz}$ ✓

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

### Solution to B(a) — Four Topologies (Diagrammatic + Tabular)

The four topologies arise from two choices at input (series vs shunt mixing) and two at output (voltage vs current sampling).

**1. Voltage-Series (Series-Shunt):** Samples output voltage; mixes feedback voltage in series with source. Acts as a **VCVS / voltage amplifier**. $A_{vf} = A_v / (1 + A_v \beta)$. High $R_i$, low $R_o$.

**2. Voltage-Shunt (Shunt-Shunt):** Samples output voltage; mixes feedback current in shunt (parallel) with source. Acts as a **transresistance amplifier** ($V_o/I_s$). Low $R_i$, low $R_o$. $R_{mf} = R_m/(1 + R_m \beta)$.

**3. Current-Series (Series-Series):** Samples output current; mixes feedback voltage in series. Acts as a **transconductance amplifier** ($I_o/V_s$). High $R_i$, high $R_o$. $G_{mf} = G_m/(1 + G_m \beta)$.

**4. Current-Shunt (Shunt-Series):** Samples output current; mixes feedback current in shunt. Acts as a **CCCS / current amplifier** ($I_o/I_s$). Low $R_i$, high $R_o$. $A_{if} = A_i/(1 + A_i \beta)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tabular summary is given in Section 2.4 of these notes — students are advised to reproduce it in the exam.**

### Solution to B(b) — Voltage-Series Numerical

**Given:** $A_v = 500$, $\beta = 0.04$, $R_i = 4\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 80\text{ }\Omega$, $BW = 200\text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1:** Desensitization factor:
$$1 + A_v \beta = 1 + 500 \times 0.04 = 1 + 20 = 21$$

**Step 2:** Closed-loop voltage gain:
$$A_{vf} = \frac{500}{21} = 23.81\;\text{V/V}$$

**Step 3:** Input resistance (series → boosted):
$$R_{if} = 4\text{ k}\Omega \times 21 = 84\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Output resistance (voltage sampling → reduced):
$$R_{of} = \frac{80}{21} \approx 3.81\;\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Reduction in gain:
$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{A_v - A_{vf}}{A_v} \times 100\% = \frac{500 - 23.81}{500} \times 100\% \approx 95.24\%$$

**Step 6:** Bandwidth extension:
$$BW_f = 200\text{ kHz} \times 21 = 4.2\text{ MHz}$$

**Comment:** The voltage gain drops by ~95 %, but the input resistance increases 21×, the output resistance drops 21×, and the bandwidth increases 21× — a textbook demonstration of the **gain-bandwidth tradeoff** and the impedance-shaping power of negative feedback.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Feedback Amplifier Problems (KTU Board Examiner Patterns):**
> 1. **Wrong topology identification** — students often confuse the *sampling type* with the *mixing type*. Remember: *output sampling decides the output resistance*; *input mixing decides the input resistance*.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of $\beta$** — in voltage-series, $\beta$ is **dimensionless**; in voltage-shunt, $\beta$ has units of $\Omega$ (V/A); in current-series, $\beta$ has units of S (A/V); in current-shunt, $\beta$ is **dimensionless**.
> 3. **Confusing $A$ with $A_f$ in sensitivity derivation** — the desensitization factor $(1+A\beta)$ uses the **open-loop** $A$ and $\beta$, not the closed-loop quantities.
> 4. **GBP verification skipped** — many students compute $A_f$ and $BW_f$ but forget to verify the product matches $A \cdot BW$, losing easy marks.
> 5. **Sign error in summing junction** — writing $V_{\epsilon} = V_s + V_f$ instead of $V_s - V_f$ leads to a positive feedback equation and a non-physical answer. Always confirm NFB means **subtraction**.
> 6. **Failing to state the type of ideal amplifier** — in part (a) of B-type questions, students describe the topology but forget to name it as VCVS, VCCS, CCCS, or CCVS, losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Feedback Amplifiers**

- The **fundamental closed-loop gain** is $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$, applicable to **every** feedback topology.
- The **loop gain** $A\beta$ governs *all* feedback properties: stability, bandwidth, distortion, and impedance.
- **Negative feedback trades gain for quality**: gain ↓ by $(1+A\beta)$, bandwidth ↑ by $(1+A\beta)$, distortion ↓ by $(1+A\beta)$, GBP = constant.
- The **four basic topologies** are formed by 2 × 2 combinations of input mixing and output sampling:
  - **Voltage-Series** (VCVS): high $R_i$, low $R_o$ → voltage amplifier.
  - **Voltage-Shunt** (V-I): low $R_i$, low $R_o$ → transresistance amplifier.
  - **Current-Series** (I-S): high $R_i$, high $R_o$ → transconductance amplifier.
  - **Current-Shunt** (I-I): low $R_i$, high $R_o$ → current amplifier.
- **Resistance transformation rules (must memorize):**
  - Series input → $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ (increases)
  - Shunt input → $R_{if} = R_i/(1+A\beta)$ (decreases)
  - Voltage output → $R_{of} = R_o/(1+A\beta)$ (decreases)
  - Current output → $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ (increases)
- **Deep feedback** ($A\beta \gg 1$) collapses $A_f \approx 1/\beta$: gain is set by the **passive** feedback network → predictable, accurate, insensitive to active device parameters.
- The **feedback factor $\beta$** is obtained by setting the appropriate input port of the feedback network:
  - Open-circuit for series mixing (no current flows into $\beta$).
  - Short-circuit for shunt mixing (no voltage develops across $\beta$ input).
- The **summing junction convention** for NFB is **always subtraction**: $X_{\epsilon} = X_s - X_f$.
- **Bandwidth and gain trade-off** is the central design lever — for a fixed GBP, you can either have high gain over a narrow band or moderate gain over a wide band.
- **Distortion** and **noise injected at the basic amplifier** are reduced by $(1+A\beta)$, but **noise on the source** is not reduced.
- **Stability is not automatic** — multiple poles in $A(j\omega)$ can cause oscillation if the phase of $A\beta$ reaches 180° before $|A\beta|$ drops to unity (Nyquist criterion). This motivates compensation, covered in the next module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sinusoidal Oscillators: Criterion for Oscillation, Basic Principles of RC and Crystal Oscillators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sinusoidal Oscillators: Criterion for Oscillation, Basic Principles of RC and Crystal Oscillators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sinusoidal Oscillator?

A **Sinusoidal Oscillator** is an electronic circuit that generates a continuous, periodic, sinusoidal (pure sine wave) output signal without requiring any external input signal. The energy conversion occurs entirely from a DC power supply to an AC sinusoidal output waveform, governed by the energy-storing elements and feedback network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An oscillator is a self-sustaining electronic circuit that delivers an output signal whose frequency, waveform, and amplitude are determined by the circuit components themselves, with no external excitation (except the DC power supply).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine pushing a child on a swing:
- The **child + swing** system is the **tank circuit / resonant circuit** (stores and exchanges energy).
- Each push you give at the right time is the **feedback signal**.
- Your muscle power is the **DC supply**.
- If you push at exactly the right moment (correct phase) with enough energy (correct gain), the swing keeps going forever — this is **sustained oscillation**.

If you push at the wrong time or too softly, the swing eventually stops — this is **damped oscillation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A sinusoidal oscillator must satisfy two conditions simultaneously: the **Barkhausen Criterion (gain condition)** and the **phase condition (0° or 360° loop phase shift)**.

## 1.3 The Two Major Families of Sinusoidal Oscillators

| Family | Frequency Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **RC Oscillators** (Low Frequency) | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** | Audio frequency generation, function generators, lab equipment |
| **LC Oscillators** (High Frequency) | **> 1 MHz to GHz** | RF transmitters, communication systems |
| **Crystal Oscillators** (Ultra Stable) | **Fixed frequency** (kHz to 100+ MHz) | Clocks in microcontrollers, watches, communication carriers |

In Module 3, we focus on the **Criterion for Oscillation** and the basic principles of **RC** and **Crystal** oscillators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Barkhausen Criterion (Criterion for Oscillation)

The **Barkhausen Criterion** is the foundational mathematical condition that any sinusoidal oscillator MUST satisfy to produce sustained, self-generated oscillations.

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram of a Feedback Oscillator

Every sinusoidal oscillator can be modelled as an **amplifier** with a **positive feedback network**.

Let:
- $A$ = Voltage gain of the amplifier block (forward path)
- $\beta$ = Feedback fraction of the feedback network (reverse path)
- $V_{in}$ = Input voltage to the amplifier
- $V_{out}$ = Output voltage of the amplifier
- $V_f$ = Feedback voltage

The feedback voltage is:
$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

For sustained oscillation, the feedback voltage must be **exactly equal** in magnitude and **in phase** with the original input voltage. That is:
$$V_f = V_{in}$$

### 2.1.2 Derivation of the Loop Gain Condition

The closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier is given by the standard expression:
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

For oscillation, the amplifier should ideally have **infinite gain** ($A_f \to \infty$), which mathematically requires the denominator to vanish:
$$1 - A\beta = 0$$

$$\boxed{A\beta = +1}$$

This is the **Barkhausen Criterion** in its compact form.

## 2.2 The Two Barkhausen Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition 1 — Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity.
> $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition 2 — Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be **0°** (or an integer multiple of 360°).
> $$\angle A\beta = 0^\circ + n \cdot 360^\circ, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

## 2.3 Practical Interpretation: Why $|A\beta| = 1$ is Not Enough

In a real oscillator, when the circuit is first switched on, thermal noise provides a tiny initial signal. For the oscillation to **build up from zero**, the loop gain must initially be **greater than 1**:
$$A\beta > 1 \text{ (start-up condition)}$$

As the amplitude grows, the non-linearity of the active device (transistor/Op-Amp) reduces the effective gain until a steady state is reached where:
$$A\beta = 1 \text{ (steady-state condition)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This automatic amplitude stabilization is called **amplitude limiting**, and it is achieved through the inherent non-linearity of the amplifying device — the transistor's saturation/cutoff, or an Op-Amp's slew rate limiting.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Condition / Variable | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Barkhausen Magnitude | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Steady-state loop gain | Dimensionless |
| Barkhausen Phase | $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ or $360^\circ$ | Total phase shift | Degrees |
| Start-up Condition | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | Initial build-up | Dimensionless |
| Closed-loop Gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$ | For $A\beta \to 1$, $A_f \to \infty$ | Dimensionless |
| RC Phase-Shift Osc. Freq. | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | 3 RC sections, CE/BJT amp | Hz |
| Wien Bridge Osc. Freq. | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | For $R_1=R_2=R$, $C_1=C_2=C$: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| Crystal Series Resonance | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_s C_s}}$ | Series resonant frequency | Hz |
| Crystal Parallel Resonance | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_s \cdot \dfrac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}}}$ | Parallel resonant frequency | Hz |
| Quality Factor (Crystal) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{L_s}{C_s}}$ | Extremely high (10,000 to 100,000) | Dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RC Oscillators** are the heart of every laboratory function generator and audio test bench.
- **Crystal Oscillators** time every digital device on Earth — your laptop, smartphone, and even the GPS satellites.
- The **Barkhausen Criterion** is the universal design checklist for ALL sinusoidal oscillator circuits, regardless of topology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Barkhausen Criterion

### Step 1 — Setup the Feedback Loop

The fundamental feedback configuration has an amplifier with gain $A$ and a feedback network with transfer function $\beta$.

Without feedback, the output is:
$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$

### Step 2 — Feedback Signal Generation

A portion $\beta$ of the output is fed back to the input:
$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

### Step 3 — Effective Input

In a positive feedback system, the feedback signal reinforces the input:
$$V_{in, \text{effective}} = V_{in} + V_f = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$$

### Step 4 — Write the Output Equation

$$V_{out} = A \cdot (V_{in} + \beta V_{out})$$

### Step 5 — Expand and Rearrange

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in} + A\beta \cdot V_{out}$$

$$V_{out} - A\beta \cdot V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} (1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$

### Step 6 — Closed-Loop Gain

$$A_f = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

### Step 7 — Oscillation Condition

For self-sustained oscillation, the circuit must produce a finite output with **zero external input** ($V_{in} = 0$). From the relation $V_{out}(1 - A\beta) = A V_{in}$, this is only possible if:

$$1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{A\beta = 1}$$

In phasor form, this splits into the **magnitude** and **phase** conditions:
$$|A\beta| = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ n\text{)}$$

---

## 3.2 RC Phase-Shift Oscillator — Frequency of Oscillation

The RC phase-shift oscillator uses a Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier (which inverts — provides 180°) followed by a **three-stage RC ladder network** that provides another 180° of phase shift, satisfying the total 360° loop requirement.

### Step 1 — Frequency-Dependent Phase Shift Per Section

Each RC section contributes a phase lead/lag given by:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2}\right)$$

(For 3 identical sections, each provides 60° at the oscillation frequency.)

### Step 2 — The Loop Gain Equation

The transfer function of a 3-section RC network (all R, C identical) is:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5(\omega RC)^2 + j\left[6(\omega RC) - (\omega RC)^3\right]}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Phase Condition

For the imaginary part of the denominator to be zero (purely real, hence zero phase):
$$6(\omega RC) - (\omega RC)^3 = 0$$

$$(\omega RC)^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega RC = \sqrt{6}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Oscillation Frequency

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$$

### Step 5 — Magnitude Condition

Substituting back:
$$|\beta| = \frac{1}{29}$$

Since the CE amplifier provides $|A| = \dfrac{h_{fe} R_C}{R_1 + R_E}$ (with emitter bias), the magnitude condition requires:
$$|A\beta| = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad |A| = 29$$

This means the BJT must have $h_{fe} \geq 29$ to sustain oscillation.

---

## 3.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Frequency of Oscillation

The Wien Bridge oscillator uses a **non-inverting Op-Amp** amplifier (0° phase) with a Wien Bridge feedback network (0° at one specific frequency).

### Step 1 — Wien Network Transfer Function

The Wien network consists of a series RC arm ($R_1$, $C_1$) and a parallel RC arm ($R_2$, $C_2$).

Let $Z_s = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}$ and $Z_p = \dfrac{R_2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}}$

The transfer function is:
$$\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

### Step 2 — Simplification with Equal Components

Setting $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

After expanding and grouping the real and imaginary parts, the condition for the phase of $\beta$ to be zero gives:
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

### Step 3 — Final Frequency Formula

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

### Step 4 — At Resonance, $\beta = 1/3$

At the oscillation frequency, the Wien network transfer function magnitude is exactly:
$$|\beta| = \frac{1}{3}$$

The non-inverting Op-Amp provides:
$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g}$$

Applying the magnitude condition $|A\beta| = 1$:
$$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 2$$

So the Op-Amp must have a closed-loop gain of exactly **3** to sustain oscillation.

---

## 3.4 Crystal Oscillator — Equivalent Circuit and Resonant Frequencies

### Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit of a Quartz Crystal

A quartz crystal is modelled as:
- **$L_s$** (motional inductance) — typically 0.001 to 10 H
- **$C_s$** (motional capacitance) — typically 0.01 to 100 pF (very small)
- **$R_s$** (motional resistance) — series loss, very small
- **$C_p$** (shunt/holder capacitance) — typically 1 to 10 pF

### Step 2 — Series Resonance

When $X_{L_s} = X_{C_s}$, the $L_s$–$C_s$ branch behaves as a **short circuit**. Resolving:
$$\omega_s L_s = \frac{1}{\omega_s C_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_s = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_s C_s}}$$

$$\boxed{f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_s C_s}}}$$

### Step 3 — Parallel Resonance

When the impedance of the entire network becomes maximum (open circuit like):
$$\omega_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_s \cdot \dfrac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}}}$$

$$\boxed{f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_s \cdot \dfrac{C_s C_p}{C_s + C_p}}}}$$

### Step 4 — The Narrow Operating Band

Since $C_p \gg C_s$ typically, the parallel capacitance is approximately $C_s$, giving:
$$f_p \approx f_s \left(1 + \frac{C_s}{2C_p}\right)$$

The gap $f_p - f_s$ is **very small** (often < 1% of $f_s$), which is why crystal oscillators have such high frequency stability.

> [!NOTE]
> In the small band between $f_s$ and $f_p$, the crystal behaves as an **inductor**, which is exactly what is needed in a Pierce oscillator to form an LC tank with an external capacitor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Feedback Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[DC Supply] --> nodeB[Active Amplifier A]
    nodeC[DC Bias Network] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[Output Vout]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Feedback Network Beta]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Feedback Voltage Vf]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeG[Sustained Sine Wave Output]
```

## 4.2 Barkhausen Criterion Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Start: Design Oscillator] --> stepB[Choose Active Device and Topology]
    stepB --> stepC[Loop Phase = 0 or 360 degrees]
    stepC --> stepC1{Phase Condition Met}
    stepC1 -->|No| stepC2[Modify Feedback Network]
    stepC2 --> stepC
    stepC1 -->|Yes| stepD[Loop Magnitude A beta = 1]
    stepD --> stepD1{Magnitude Condition Met}
    stepD1 -->|No| stepD2[Adjust Amplifier Gain]
    stepD2 --> stepD
    stepD1 -->|Yes| stepE[Oscillator Sustains]
    stepE --> stepF[Check Amplitude Stability]
    stepF --> stepG[Sine Wave Output Generated]
```

## 4.3 RC Phase-Shift Oscillator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[DC Supply] --> step2[BJT CE Amplifier 180 deg]
    step2 --> step3[Output across Collector Resistor]
    step3 --> step4[RC Section 1: 60 deg]
    step4 --> step5[RC Section 2: 60 deg]
    step5 --> step6[RC Section 3: 60 deg]
    step6 --> step7[Feedback to Base of BJT]
    step7 --> step2
    step6 --> step8[Total Loop Phase = 360 deg]
    step8 --> step9[Sine Wave at f0]
```

## 4.4 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[DC Supply +/-V] --> step2[Non-Inverting Op-Amp Gain 3]
    step2 --> step3[Output Vout Sine Wave]
    step3 --> step4[Series RC Arm Zs]
    step3 --> step5[Parallel RC Arm Zp]
    step4 --> step6[Wien Network Beta]
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7[Feedback to Non-Inverting Input]
    step7 --> step2
    step6 --> step8[Resonance at f0 = 1 over 2 pi RC]
    step8 --> step9[Zero Phase Condition Met]
    step9 --> step10[Stable Sine Wave at f0]
```

## 4.5 Quartz Crystal Equivalent Circuit and Resonance Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Terminal 1] --> A2[Rs Series]
    A2 --> A3[Ls Inductance]
    A3 --> A4[Cs Capacitance]
    A4 --> A5[Terminal 2]
    A5 --> A6[Cp Shunt Capacitance]
    A6 --> A1
    A1 --> B1[Series Resonance fs]
    A1 --> B2[Parallel Resonance fp]
    B1 --> C1[Crystal acts as SHORT]
    B2 --> C2[Crystal acts as INDUCTOR]

```

## 4.6 Pierce Crystal Oscillator Block Architecture

```mermaid

    start[DC Supply] --> amp[Inverting Amplifier]

    gateX --> amp
    amp --> cd1[Capacitor C1 at Gate]
    amp --> cd2[Capacitor C2 at Drain]
    cd1 --> ground1[Ground]
    cd2 --> ground2[Ground]
    amp --> output[Stable Sine Wave at fp]
```
