# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a differential amplifier. Why is it expressed in decibels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **CMRR** is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the differential gain to the magnitude of the common-mode gain of a differential amplifier:
>
> $$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$
>
> An ideal differential amplifier must have $A_c = 0$ (reject all common-mode signals like noise) and finite $A_d$ (amplify the differential signal). Hence CMRR is a measure of how well the amplifier discriminates the desired differential signal from unwanted common-mode interference.
>
> Because $A_d$ and $A_c$ may differ by several orders of magnitude, the ratio is **expressed logarithmically in decibels** for convenient comparison:
>
> $$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$
>
> A higher CMRR (in dB) means better noise rejection — typical op-amps specify 70 dB – 120 dB.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by common-mode and differential-mode input signals? Give one example of each in a real measurement system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> When two input signals $v_1$ and $v_2$ are applied at the bases of a differential pair, they can be decomposed into:
>
> - **Differential-mode input:** $v_d = v_1 - v_2$ — the *difference* of the two signals.
> - **Common-mode input:** $v_{cm} = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ — the *average* of the two signals.
>
> **Example (differential):** In an **ECG machine**, the bio-electric potential difference between the left-arm and right-arm electrodes (typically ~ 1 mV) is the desired differential signal.
>
> **Example (common-mode):** The **50 Hz mains hum** picked up equally by both ECG electrodes (and the patient's body acting as an antenna) is a common-mode signal. A good ECG front-end rejects it via high CMRR.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a BJT differential amplifier when a **differential-mode input** $v_d$ is applied. Derive the expression for the differential-mode gain $A_d$ in terms of $g_m$ and $R_C$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the differential amplifier in part (a), if a **common-mode input** $v_{cm}$ is applied, derive the expression for the common-mode gain $A_c$ and hence obtain the **CMRR** of the circuit. State two methods to improve CMRR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Draw the circuit** (refer to Section 4.3 ASCII schematic).
A symmetric BJT pair Q1, Q2 with $R_{C1} = R_{C2} = R_C$, tail resistor $R_E$ to $-V_{EE}$. Inputs $v_1$ and $v_2$ at the bases; outputs $v_{o1}$ and $v_{o2}$ at the collectors.

**Step 2 — Apply the differential input** as $v_1 = +v_d/2$, $v_2 = -v_d/2$.

**Step 3 — Virtual ground argument.** By symmetry, the common emitter node remains at constant AC potential, hence it acts as a **virtual AC ground** for differential signals.

**Step 4 — Half-circuit analysis.** The signal $v_d/2$ drives the base of Q1 in a grounded-emitter configuration:

$$i_{c1} = g_m \cdot \frac{v_d}{2}$$

$$v_{o1} = -i_{c1} R_C = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2} v_d$$

**Step 5 — By symmetry** $v_{o2} = +\frac{g_m R_C}{2} v_d$ (Q2 driven by $-v_d/2$).

**Step 6 — Single-ended gain:** $A_{d1} = v_{o1}/v_d = -g_m R_C / 2$.

**Step 7 — Double-ended (differential) gain:**

$$\boxed{A_d = \frac{v_{o1} - v_{o2}}{v_d} = -g_m R_C}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply common-mode input** $v_1 = v_2 = v_{cm}$. By symmetry, both collectors see the same disturbance.

**Step 2 — Effective degeneration.** The shared tail $R_E$ appears as $2 R_E$ to each transistor (current doubles in the tail).

**Step 3 — Using the degenerated CE gain formula:**

$$A_c = -\frac{g_m R_C}{1 + 2 g_m R_E} \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_E} \quad (\text{for } R_E \gg r_e)$$

**Step 4 — CMRR:**

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert \approx \frac{R_E}{r_e} = g_m R_E}$$

**Step 5 — In dB:**

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{R_E}{r_e} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Two methods to improve CMRR:**

1. **Replace the tail resistor $R_E$ with a constant-current source (active load).** The current source has nearly infinite small-signal output impedance, making $A_c \to 0$ and $\text{CMRR} \to \infty$ ideally.
2. **Use matched (monolithic) transistor pairs** so that $\beta$ and $V_{BE}$ of Q1 and Q2 are as close as possible. Mismatches convert common-mode to differential signals and degrade CMRR.
3. **(Bonus)** Use a **current-mirror load** for active-loading, which simultaneously boosts $A_d$ and improves CMRR.

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`** *(Alternative to Question A)*

> **(a)** For an emitter-coupled BJT differential pair with $V_{CC} = +10\ \text{V}$, $-V_{EE} = -10\ \text{V}$, $R_C = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, calculate **(i)** the quiescent collector current $I_C$, **(ii)** the transconductance $g_m$, and **(iii)** the differential gain $A_d$ (double-ended output). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the same circuit, find the **common-mode gain** $A_c$ (single-ended) and the **CMRR in dB**. If the input signal is $v_1 = 50\ \text{mV}$ and $v_2 = 49\ \text{mV}$, find the **differential output** $v_{od}$ and the **common-mode output** $v_{oc}$ (single-ended). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**(i) Quiescent collector current:**

$$I_{EE} = \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_E} = \frac{10 - 0.7}{10000} = \frac{9.3}{10000} = 0.93\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2} = \frac{0.93}{2} = 0.465\ \text{mA}$$

**(ii) Transconductance:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{0.465\ \text{mA}}{25\ \text{mV}} = 18.6\ \text{mA/V} = 0.0186\ \text{S}$$

**(iii) Differential gain (double-ended):**

$$A_d = -g_m R_C = -0.0186 \times 5000 = -93\ \text{V/V}$$

$$|A_d| = 93$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Common-mode gain (single-ended, approximate):**

$$A_c \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_E} = -\frac{5000}{2 \times 10000} = -0.25\ \text{V/V}$$

**CMRR (ratio and dB):**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{|A_d|}{|A_c|} = \frac{93}{0.25} = 372$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(372) = 20 \times 2.5705 = 51.4\ \text{dB}$$

**Differential and common-mode components of the input:**

$$v_d = v_1 - v_2 = 50 - 49 = 1\ \text{mV}$$

$$v_{cm} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} = \frac{50 + 49}{2} = 49.5\ \text{mV}$$

**Differential output (double-ended):**

$$v_{od} = A_d \cdot v_d = -93 \times 1\ \text{mV} = -93\ \text{mV}$$

**Common-mode output (single-ended):**

$$v_{oc} = A_c \cdot v_{cm} = -0.25 \times 49.5\ \text{mV} \approx -12.4\ \text{mV}$$

This shows that although the input differential signal is only 1 mV, it produces a 93 mV differential output — while the much larger 49.5 mV common-mode input only produces 12.4 mV common-mode output (which is *rejected* when we use the differential output $v_{od}$).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on differential-amplifier questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 between single-ended and double-ended gain.** Double-ended differential gain is $g_m R_C$ (NOT $g_m R_C / 2$). Double-ended common-mode gain is **zero** for an ideal symmetric pair. KTU examiners check this carefully.
> 2. **Using $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$ instead of $25\ \text{mV}$.** Either is acceptable, but you **must state which you used**. The KTU preferred value is **25 mV**.
> 3. **Mixing up the tail current.** $I_{EE}$ is the *total* tail current, NOT the per-transistor current. $I_C = I_{EE}/2$.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert CMRR to dB.** Always express the final answer in **dB** unless the problem explicitly says otherwise.
> 5. **Sign errors in the gain formula.** $A_d = -g_m R_C$ (negative). The negative sign matters when you compute the phase. Single-ended $A_{d1} = -g_m R_C / 2$ is also negative.
> 6. **Using the *approximate* $A_c$ formula when $R_E$ is not large.** If $R_E$ is comparable to $r_e$, the approximation $A_c = -R_C/(2R_E)$ breaks down. Use the exact form $A_c = -g_m R_C/(1 + 2 g_m R_E)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — BJT Differential Pair**

- ☐ The pair has **two matched BJTs** with a **common emitter** and a **tail element** (resistor or current source).
- ☐ Quiescent current: $I_C = (V_{EE} - V_{BE}) / (2 R_E)$.
- ☐ Per-transistor transconductance: $g_m = I_C / V_T$.
- ☐ Differential gain (double-ended): $A_d = -g_m R_C = -R_C / r_e$.
- ☐ Differential gain (single-ended): $A_{d1} = -g_m R_C / 2$.
- ☐ Common-mode gain (single-ended, with $R_E$): $A_c \approx -R_C / (2 R_E)$.
- ☐ Common-mode gain with **ideal current-source tail**: $A_c = 0$ (infinite CMRR ideally).
- ☐ **CMRR (ratio):** $\vert A_d / A_c \vert$.
- ☐ **CMRR (dB):** $20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d / A_c \vert)$.
- ☐ Approximate CMRR with resistor tail: $\text{CMRR} \approx R_E / r_e$.
- ☐ Virtual AC ground at the common emitter node is **only valid in differential mode**, not common mode.
- ☐ Common-mode input range is bounded by transistor saturation limits: $V_{EE} + V_{CE(sat)} + V_{BE} \leq v_{cm} \leq V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)} + V_{BE}$.
- ☐ To **improve CMRR**: (i) use a **current-source tail**, (ii) use **matched monolithic transistors**, (iii) use **active (current-mirror) loads**.
- ☐ Differential input resistance: $R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$. Common-mode input resistance is much larger: $R_{ic} = [r_\pi + 2(\beta+1) R_E]/2$.
- ☐ The differential pair is the **input stage of every op-amp** — op-amp CMRR inherits from this stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this checklist and the formulas in the table of Section 2.3, and you will be able to solve any KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic — Part A, Part B, or numerical — confidently.**

---
